feel: straight four-on-the-floor; classic Chicago tracks are mostly straight 16ths, but a light MPC-style swing (~54-58%, shuffle-67 template) on closed hats/percussion is idiomatic for the groovier UK/909 variants. The hypnotic feel comes from a perfectly-locked grid against the continuously morphing 303 filter, not from swing.

scalesModes: Natural minor (Aeolian) - by far the default
Minor pentatonic - the safest note pool for 303 lines
Phrygian / Phrygian-dominant - for a darker, more 'Eastern' acid flavour
Harmonic minor - occasional, for the raised-7th tension
chordTypes: Often NO chords at all - acid house is frequently a single-note 303 line plus drums, 'exploration of texture preferred over melody'
Minor triads and minor7 'organ/piano stabs' when chords appear
Detroit-style minor 7th and minor 9th stabs (string/brass machine)
typicalProgressions: i (static one-chord modal vamp - the most common 'progression' is no progression: a single tonic riff looped)
i - VI - VII (Aeolian rise)
i - VII - VI - VII
i - iv (minor plagal stab pattern)
i - VI - III - VII (when it borrows from deep/Detroit house)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Very slow to none. Most tracks sit on a single tonic key for the whole track; when stabs are present they typically change every 1, 2 or 4 bars. Harmonic motion is deliberately minimal so the ear focuses on the filter-sweeping 303 and the drum groove.

form: Loop-based and arrangement-by-subtraction/addition rather than verse-chorus. Foundation is a 1- or 2-bar drum+303 loop. Canonical structure: (1) Intro - drums build up element by element (kick, then hats, then clap) over 8-16 bars; (2) drop the 303 in and let it run; (3) the 'arrangement' is performed live by tweaking 303 CUTOFF/RESONANCE/ENV-MOD/DECAY knobs over many bars - the filter opening up is the build; (4) breakdowns strip to drums or to the bare 303; (5) re-introduce full groove. Tracks are long (5-12 min; Phuture's 'Acid Tracks' ran ~12 min) and DJ-friendly with 16-32 bar intros/outros of just drums. A 303-line trick: make the sequence an odd number of steps (15 or 13) against the 16-step drum loop so the two slowly drift in and out of phase, generating evolving variation from static material.

bass.character: The defining 'squelch': a liquid, resonant, vocal-like single-oscillator bass that morphs continuously. Low-mid-forward, mono, often distorted/saturated so the resonant filter peak screams. Notes are tied together by glides and punched out by accents, producing the wiggling, talking quality.
bass.signatureTechnique: Per-step SLIDE and ACCENT programming. SLIDE = legato portamento that ties the current step to the next with a fixed-rate pitch glide and no re-trigger of the gate (this is THE 303 move); idiomatic slides are a downward octave, or an octave-plus-semitone leap, often with the accent on the first note. ACCENT = simultaneously boosts that step's volume AND opens the filter cutoff (via the accent envelope) so accented notes 'pop' brighter - it is not just velocity. Octave jumps (+12 on scattered steps) plus slides create the bounce.
bass.synthesis: Roland TB-303 (or clone/emulation): ONE oscillator, sawtooth (thin/aggressive, the acid default) or square (rounder); 18 dB/oct (3-pole) resonant lowpass filter; single decay envelope feeding both filter (ENV MOD amount) and a separate accent path. Recipe: pick saw, set RESONANCE high (~70-90%), start CUTOFF low and slowly open it, ENV MOD moderate-to-high so each note has a downward filter pluck, DECAY short-to-medium controls how fast the squelch closes, ACCENT knob ~50-80%. Run the output through overdrive/distortion or tube saturation - the distortion interacting with the resonant peak is essential to the canonical sound. Then real-time-automate CUTOFF and RESONANCE for the 'performance'.

lead.character: Acid house usually has no distinct lead instrument - the 303 IS the lead, bass and hook simultaneously. When a secondary 'lead' appears it is a second 303 line, a filtered acid arp, a sparse Detroit-style synth-brass/string stab, or processed vocal drop-ins ('Acieeed', spoken phrases) used percussively rather than melodically.
lead.synthesis: If a real lead is wanted: a second monophonic TB-303-type voice an octave up with its own slides/accents, or a simple analog mono-synth (saw/square) through a heavily resonant lowpass with tempo-synced delay. Melodies stay short, modal (minor/pentatonic), and repetitive.

pads.character: Sparse-to-absent. When present: cold, minimal sustained string/organ washes or 'synthetic strings and stabs, usually minimal' (Wikipedia). Used for atmosphere in breakdowns more than as a harmonic engine. Long reverb tails, low-passed, sitting behind the 303.
pads.synthesis: String-machine or analog-poly-style: stacked detuned saw oscillators (or a Juno-style chorused saw pad), slow attack, lowpass filtered, large hall/cathedral reverb with low-frequency roll-off above ~400 Hz so it doesn't clutter the sub. Kept quiet and atmospheric; in many classic tracks there is no pad at all.

drums.machine: Roland TR-909 (the canonical house/acid machine: punchy analog kick with tunable attack/decay, analog noise snare with tone+snappy, analog clap, and 6-bit sampled hi-hats/ride/crash) and/or TR-808 (deeper boomy sine kick, tighter handclap, sizzly hats). TR-707/727 also appear. Original Phuture 'Acid Tracks' used an 808; the harder UK/909 sound dominates the broader genre. Sounds are typically distorted/compressed.
drums.kick: Four-on-the-floor: one kick on every quarter note. 909-style with shortish decay and punchy attack so it doesn't mask the 303 sub. Tuned low; sometimes a tiny pitch-blip on attack.
drums.snare: Backbeat clap and/or snare on beats 2 and 4. In 909 tracks the clap is the primary backbeat (often clap+snare layered for the phased noise effect, since the 909 clap and snare share a noise source). Light ghost/syncopated snare hits added in the 2nd bar of a 2-bar loop for variation.
drums.hats: Closed hi-hats drive the 8ths or 16ths; the signature gesture is an OPEN hi-hat on every off-beat (the '+' of each beat), especially a prominent open hat on the off-beat of 4 just before the loop wraps to lift energy. Closed-hat 16ths give the propulsive shimmer; occasional open-hat accents add motion.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step grid, steps numbered 1-16 (beats on 1,5,9,13). KICK: 1,5,9,13 (four-on-floor). CLAP/SNARE (backbeat): 5,13 (i.e. beats 2 and 4). CLOSED HAT: every step 1-16 for a busy 16th drive, OR offbeat-8ths on 3,7,11,15 for a sparser feel. OPEN HAT: 3,7,11,15 (every off-beat 8th) - or, for the classic minimal version, open hat only on 7 and 15 (the '+' of 2 and 4), with the open hat on 15 lifting into the loop. Optional ghost snare/perc: 8,16 in alternate bars; optional rim/shaker on 16ths for groove. Apply ~54-58% swing to closed hats/perc for the grooved variant; leave straight for the raw Chicago variant.

signatureSounds: TB-303 'acid squelch' bassline: sawtooth osc -> resonant 3-pole lowpass (high resonance, low-opening cutoff, env-mod pluck) with per-step slide+accent, run through distortion. This single sound IS the genre.
Filter-sweep performance: slowly opening/closing the 303 CUTOFF and riding RESONANCE over many bars as the main arrangement device
909 four-on-the-floor: punchy analog kick + clap-backbeat + open-hat-on-the-offbeat
808 boom kick + tight 808 handclap (the original Phuture flavour)
Octave-jump + downward-octave-slide 303 melodic figures
Sparse vocal/spoken drop-ins used percussively ('Acieeed', 'I've lost control')
Tempo-synced (dotted-1/8) delay on the 303 so notes 'chase each other', plus cavernous reverb in breakdowns
identityMustHaves: A resonant, slide-and-accent-driven TB-303 (or faithful clone/emulation) squelch bassline - non-negotiable; without it it is just house
Four-on-the-floor kick at ~118-130 BPM in 4/4 from an 808/909-type machine
Real-time filter movement (cutoff/resonance automation) as the primary source of evolution over a static, looping, single-key/modal foundation
Hypnotic repetition with minimal-to-no harmonic change - texture and groove prioritised over melody/chords
engineRequirements: Monosynth voice with TRUE 303 behaviour: per-step SLIDE (legato glide that suppresses gate re-trigger and portamentos at a fixed rate to the next step) and per-step ACCENT (boosts both amplitude AND filter cutoff via a separate accent envelope, not just velocity)
Resonant lowpass filter (ideally 18 dB/oct) with a dedicated decay envelope whose modulation depth (ENV MOD) and decay time are controllable, producing the per-note filter pluck
Automatable/sequence-able CUTOFF and RESONANCE that can be modulated over the course of the arrangement (LFO or recorded automation lanes) - the 'performance' filter sweep
Waveform select per-303 voice (saw vs square)
A distortion/overdrive/saturation stage AFTER the filter (interaction with the resonant peak is essential)
16-step step sequencer with per-step note, gate/rest, octave-up/down, slide flag and accent flag
Variable pattern length independent of the drum loop (support 13/15/16/odd step lengths) so the 303 can phase against the 4/4 drums

Drum step-sequencer with 808/909-style synthesized/sampled voices: tunable analog-style kick (attack/decay), analog snare (tone/snappy), clap, and closed/open hi-hats with proper open-hat choke by the next closed hat
Optional swing/shuffle amount on hats/perc (~54-58%)
Tempo-synced delay (dotted-1/8) and reverb sends, with low-cut on reverb returns
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible and arguably one of the EASIEST electronic genres to generate convincingly, because authenticity lives in a single well-modelled instrument (the TB-303) plus a rigid four-on-the-floor grid - not in complex harmony or arrangement. The hard part is not the notes (random modal/pentatonic notes with octave jumps genuinely work - sources stress 'don't overthink it') but faithfully modelling 303 mechanics: slide as gate-suppressing fixed-rate portamento, accent as a coupled volume+cutoff boost with its own envelope, an env-mod filter pluck, and post-filter distortion that screams at the resonant peak. Get those four behaviours right and even a near-random note sequence sounds authentically acid. The other 20%: a punchy 909/808 kit, the open-hat-on-offbeat, and crucially time-varying filter automation so a static loop evolves. Risk areas: a generic resonant filter without true accent-coupling or without post distortion sounds 'house but not acid'; over-busy harmony or audible chord progressions break the genre's minimalist identity.

feel: Straight (no swing). Four-on-the-floor pulse with motion coming from 16th-note rolling bass and 1/8-dotted delays rather than from drum-grid swing. Energy is managed by long additive layering, not by groove displacement.

scalesModes: Harmonic minor color (occasional V chord tension into breakdown returns)
typicalProgressions: i-VI-III-VII (Aeolian, the canonical emotional prog-house loop, e.g. Am-F-C-G)
i-VII-VI-VII
vi-IV-I-V (festival/uplifting major-relative voicing: Am-F-C-G read as vi-IV-I-V)
i-III-VII-VI
I-V-vi-IV (major uplifting drops)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow: one chord per bar (or per 2 bars) is canonical, giving 4-bar or 8-bar harmonic loops. Chords change roughly every 2 seconds at ~126 BPM. The same loop persists across whole sections; emotion comes from re-orchestration (pad -> pluck -> supersaw stab) over a static progression, not from frequent chord changes.

form: Extended DJ-friendly arrangement, 7-9+ minutes, phrased strictly in multiples of 8 bars (8/16/32). Canonical map: Intro/mixer beats (16-32 bars, kick + hats + perc, no melody) -> first groove with rolling bass (16-32) -> Breakdown 1 (16-32 bars: kick drops out, pads/plucks/chords swell, emotional core) -> Build-up (8-16 bars: snare roll accelerating from 1/8 to 1/16 to 1/32, white-noise upriser sweep, rising filter, often a beat-skip/silence on the last bar) -> Drop/Main (16-32 bars: full supersaw + rolling bass + four-on-floor + heavy sidechain pump) -> Breakdown 2 -> second Build -> second Drop -> Outro (16-32 bars, beat-only for mixing out). Energy via gradual additive layering and filter automation, NOT shock. Transitions use white-noise sweeps, HP-filter sweeps, pitch risers/downers, and reverse-cymbal pickups on the bar before a new 8-bar phrase.

bass.character: Deep, rounded, sub-heavy rolling bass that sits below the kick. Mid-bass body so it reads on small speakers, but the fundamental is a clean sine/triangle sub. The defining trait is the pumping: it ducks hard on every kick (sidechain) and swells back up between hits, creating the breathing 'rolling' motion.
bass.signatureTechnique: Rolling 16th-note bassline. The classic rhythmic cell is 3/16 + 3/16 + 2/16 (i.e. notes on steps 1,4,7 then re-anchor), and the productive default is 'offbeat 16ths that skip the on-beat step' (steps where the kick lands are left empty/ducked so the bass fills the gaps). Mostly root-note repetition with octave jumps and quick fills/rolls between sections. Heavy kick-triggered sidechain compression with a LONGER release so the level fully recovers to unity just before the next kick (must return to zero between hits, never stay crushed).
bass.synthesis: Layered: (1) pure sine/triangle SUB for the fundamental (mono, no sidechain or lighter sidechain), (2) a saw/square MID-BASS layer for presence run through saturation, EQ'd to clear room below ~40-50 Hz and above the low-mids, sidechained to the kick. Short-ish amp decay so notes are detached and 'roll'. Glide/slide optional between repeated roots.

lead.character: Big, bright, euphoric supersaw lead/stab — the iconic festival-prog 'wall of saws'. Wide stereo, shimmering detune, lush reverb tail. Often played as rhythmic stabs locked to the chord loop in the drop, or as a sustained melodic line. Major-key drops feel anthemic; minor-key feels bittersweet.
lead.synthesis: Supersaw: 7 detuned saw oscillators (Roland JP-8000 'Super Saw' lineage), in Serum done as Saw wave with Unison 7-9 voices (odd number = smoother), detune spread ~12-30 cents (10-30c = tight/pro; more = washy), unison blend high, wide stereo (~80-100%). Stack a second osc set to '12 (1x)' octave for crisp top. For massive size, triple-stack three 9-voice unison oscillators (~27 voices) with osc2 detuned up / osc3 down. Lowpass filter (e.g. LP6/LP12) with slow filter-envelope opening on builds, cutoff near full-open in the drop, light resonance + drive. Processing chain: chorus -> EQ low-cut -> OTT/multiband upward comp (~100% then dialed back) -> plate/hall reverb. Sidechained to the kick to pump with the bass.

pads.character: Warm, wide, evolving emotional pads that carry the breakdown. Slow-attack, long-release, drenched in big reverb and 1/8-dotted delay so chords bleed into a dreamy wash. Provide the harmonic/emotional bed under which plucks and vocal chops sit. Often the emotional 'core' of the whole track.
pads.synthesis: Detuned saw or warm analog-style pad (2-3 slightly detuned saw/triangle oscillators, mild unison), very slow amp attack (200-800 ms) and long release, lowpass with a gentle slowly-opening filter automation across the breakdown. EQ: cut lows below ~150 Hz so it doesn't clash with bass, gentle high-mid lift (~1.4 kHz) for presence. Heavy reverb (large/hall, e.g. Valhalla Supermassive for the washed-out tail) + ping-pong/dotted-eighth delay. Sidechained lightly to the kick when the beat is present so it breathes with the track.

drums.machine: Hybrid: TR-909-derived kick (punchy, layered) + sampled claps and acoustic-ish hats/shakers/percussion. Kick is typically two layered samples — one for low-end body, one for transient click (low-end stripped from the click layer).
drums.kick: Four-on-the-floor: kick on every beat — steps 1, 5, 9, 13 of the 16-step bar (beats 1,2,3,4). Punchy, tuned to the track key, sidechain source for everything else.
drums.snare: No backbeat snare in the groove proper (this is house, not breaks). A CLAP carries the backbeat instead, on beats 2 and 4 (steps 5 and 13). HP-filtered ~140 Hz, often double/triple-layered with a few ms track-delay offsets so transients don't stack. A snare appears only in build-ups as an accelerating roll (1/8 -> 1/16 -> 1/32).
drums.hats: Closed hi-hat on the off-beats (the '+' of each beat): steps 3, 7, 11, 15, with subtle velocity variation (e.g. ~89/100/90/93) for groove. Open hi-hat shares the same off-beat placement to create the classic 'tss' lift. Shaker adds 16th-note motion (e.g. accents around steps 1,2,3,4 of beat one with varied velocity ~68/94/85/97).
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step bar (1=downbeat of beat 1, 5=beat 2, 9=beat 3, 13=beat 4). KICK: 1,5,9,13 (four-on-floor). CLAP/snare-role: 5,13 (backbeat on 2 and 4), HP-filtered ~140 Hz. CLOSED HAT: 3,7,11,15 (all off-beats), velocity-varied. OPEN HAT: 3,7,11,15 (off-beat, gives the open 'tss'). SHAKER/PERC: rolling 16ths layered for movement. Rolling BASS fills the steps the kick leaves open (e.g. 16th cells of 3+3+2), ducked under each kick by sidechain.
drums.other: Off-beat open hats, shakers and 16th-note percussion loops added/removed gradually to manage energy (additive arrangement). Reverse cymbals / crash on the downbeat of each new 8-bar phrase. White-noise riser and snare-roll in build-ups. Ride/tambourine layers introduced in later/bigger sections.

signatureSounds: Supersaw lead/stab: 7 detuned saw oscillators (JP-8000 lineage), ~12-30 cent unison spread, wide stereo, slow filter-env open on builds, big plate/hall reverb — the 'festival prog' wall of sound
Heavy kick-triggered sidechain pump: bass and pads duck on every kick with a long release that fully recovers before the next hit, creating the genre-defining 'breathing/pumping' motion
Rolling 16th-note bassline (3+3+2 cell / offbeat-16ths that dodge the kick), sub + saturated mid layer, octave jumps and fills between sections
Plucky arp: short-decay pluck (often built from the chord triad), arp set Up/Down at 1/16, drenched in 1/8-dotted delay + reverb for a washed-out dreamy stack
Emotional breakdown pad: slow-attack detuned-saw pad over a static i-VI-III-VII loop, huge reverb tail, low-cut ~150 Hz
Build-up FX: white-noise upriser sweep, accelerating snare roll (1/8->1/16->1/32), HP-filter sweep, pitch riser, and a beat-skip/silence on the last bar before the drop
Four-on-floor 909 kick + off-beat open hats + backbeat clap (HP ~140 Hz, layered with track-delay offsets)
identityMustHaves: Four-on-the-floor kick at ~122-128 BPM with the classic backbeat clap and off-beat open hats
Audible kick-triggered sidechain PUMP on bass and pads (the breathing motion is non-negotiable)
Long additive arrangement: extended breakdown -> rising build (snare roll + noise sweep) -> drop, phrased in 8/16/32-bar multiples (gradual development, not shock-value)
Emotional minor/Dorian harmony on a slow 1-chord-per-bar loop (i-VI-III-VII type) carried by lush pads and a bright detuned supersaw lead/pluck
engineRequirements: Sidechain ducking with per-target depth and a tunable, longish release envelope keyed to the kick (the core pump) — bass and pads must duck and recover to unity before the next kick
Supersaw oscillator: unison voice count (7-9+), detune-in-cents spread, stereo width, and optional octave-stacked sub-oscillator
Per-voice lowpass filter with an envelope/automation that slowly OPENS over a build (cutoff sweep + resonance)
16th-note step sequencer fine enough to place rolling-bass cells (3+3+2) and offbeat-16th notes that dodge the kick steps
Arpeggiator: rate (1/16), Up/Down/order modes, gate length, and a groove/velocity control
Tempo-synced delay including 1/8-dotted, plus large reverb sends (hall/plate) for the washed-out pad/pluck/lead tails
Arrangement/automation timeline that can add and remove layers in 8/16/32-bar blocks and run filter/volume automation across long breakdowns and builds
Build-up FX generators: white-noise upriser sweep, accelerating snare-roll (1/8->1/16->1/32), pitch riser/downer, reverse cymbal
Velocity per step on hats/shakers/perc for human groove, and multi-layer hits with small per-layer time offsets (clap/kick layering)
Functional-harmony aware chord engine that holds a static minor loop while re-orchestrating it across pads/plucks/stabs
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible to generate convincingly — progressive house is one of the most formulaic, rule-driven EDM genres, which is exactly what suits algorithmic generation. The four-on-floor grid, off-beat hats, backbeat clap, slow 1-chord-per-bar minor loop, and 8/16/32-bar additive arrangement are deterministic templates. The two elements that make-or-break authenticity and need real engine support are (1) the kick-triggered sidechain PUMP with correct release recovery, and (2) the supersaw (proper unison/detune/width + slowly-opening filter + big reverb) — get those wrong and it sounds like generic house. The rolling 16th bass needs a fine step grid and the kick-dodging logic. The hardest-to-fake aspect is the long-form emotional pacing of the breakdown-to-drop journey (subtle multi-minute filter/layer automation); a loop-length generator nails the groove easily but the 'epic build' arc requires explicit multi-section automation, not just a repeating bar. All sound-design recipes here are grounded in production-community consensus; specifics like exact detune cents and clap HP frequency are tutorial-typical values, not universal constants.

feel: straight (machine-quantized 16ths); driving four-on-the-floor with no swing. Rolling bass and 16th hats create forward propulsion. Optional tiny humanization (1-3 ticks) on percussion, never on the kick.

scalesModes: Natural minor (Aeolian) — default tonal center for the groove sections
Harmonic minor — the signature lead/melody scale; raised 7th gives the augmented-2nd (b6→7) 'trance interval' tension
Major (Ionian) — used for the most euphoric/uplifting anthem variants and breakdown lifts
Melodic minor — occasional, for ascending resolution lines
chordTypes: major triads (III, VI, VII, and tonic-major lifts)
sus2 and sus4 (very common on pads for openness/suspension)
power-chord/octave voicings on the supersaw lead, doubled at octave + a 3rd harmony
typicalProgressions: i-VI-III-VII (Am-F-C-G in A minor — the classic emotive trance loop)
vi-IV-I-V (relative-minor start resolving up — euphoric/uplifting variant)
i-III-VII-iv
IV-I-V-vi (expansive, 'invigorating' uplifting feel)
i-VII-VI-VII (Andalusian-flavored descending tension)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow: typically 1 chord per bar or 1 chord per 2 bars, over an 8-bar (sometimes 16-bar) repeating loop. Breakdown melodies often span an 8- or 16-bar phrase before a key/chord lift. Lead arps subdivide the slow harmony into 16th-note arpeggiated figures.

form: Long-form 7-9 min DJ arrangement. Canonical sections: Intro (~32 bars, kick + percussion + filtered rolling bass building). First groove/climax (32 bars, full beat + bass + background arps). Breakdown 1 (16-32 bars, beat & bass stripped away entirely, pads/strings + emerging lead melody, big reverb, often a key/emotional lift). Build-up (8-16 bars, rising white-noise sweep, snare/clap roll accelerating 8th→16th→32nd, filter opening, pitch riser, drum re-entry). Main drop / anthem (32 bars, full supersaw lead over rolling bass + four-on-floor — the euphoric peak). Second (shorter) breakdown + final climax, then Outro (~32 bars, beat-only for DJ mixing). Core loop length is 8 bars; phrases group in 8/16/32-bar multiples.

bass.character: Rolling, relentless, mid-forward sawtooth bass — the genre's rhythmic engine. Sits in low-mids (driving), with a separate sustained sub layer underneath for weight. Heavily sidechained to the kick.
bass.signatureTechnique: 16th-note 'rolling/gallop' bass: a note on (almost) every 16th, but the 16th that lands ON the kick (downbeats) is OMITTED and the offbeat 16ths are played, with the note immediately before/after the beat often kicked UP AN OCTAVE — the classic trance offbeat-octave gallop. Sidechain compression to the kick is mandatory (not optional) so the bass ducks on each kick and breathes back in. Notes follow the chord-progression root; move both the low and the octave-up note to match the chord.
bass.synthesis: Multi-layer stack: (1) Sub layer — single sine or filtered saw, mono, plays sustained/legato root, lowpassed, fills the very low end. (2) Mid/'rolling' layer — 1-3 detuned saw oscillators, lowpass filter with resonance, short amp decay so each 16th is a punchy 'tick', plays the offbeat-octave pattern. (3) Optional hi/'reese' layer — slightly distorted/detuned saws, high-passed, for grit/presence. All bus-compressed and sidechained together to the kick; EQ-carved so layers don't mask each other and leave room for the kick fundamental (~50-60 Hz).

lead.character: Huge, wide, euphoric supersaw 'anthem' lead — bright, detuned, shimmering, the emotional focal point of the drop. Plays arpeggiated 16th-note figures and/or sustained melodic phrases from the harmonic-minor scale, frequently doubled at the octave and harmonized in 3rds/6ths for the lush anthemic stack.
lead.synthesis: Supersaw: 7 sawtooth oscillators detuned against each other (the Roland JP-8000 'Super Saw' model), ~detune spread where voices 'just start to sound out of tune' (roughly 12-25 cents effective spread); unison/stereo width maxed for a wide image. Pro recipe stacks 3 saw oscillators each with 9-voice unison (one tuned slightly up, one slightly down → effective ~27-voice) plus a high-passed pitched-up noise oscillator for air/sizzle. Lowpass filter with a slow filter-envelope sweep opening on the drop (and automated cutoff for build sweeps); high-pass to clear low-mud. Light clip/saturation for aggression and presence. Effects chain: stereo chorus (width), 1/8 or dotted-1/8 ping-pong delay (tempo-synced), and a big, wet, slightly damped reverb. Octave + 3rd-harmony layers summed for the 'wall' sound.

pads.character: Lush, wide, sustained string/choir/synth-string pads — the harmonic 'wash' that carries the breakdown emotion and underpins the chords. Slow attack, long release, very reverberant; foreground in breakdowns, sidechain-pumped behind the beat in groove sections.
pads.synthesis: Layered detuned-saw 'string' ensemble (or sampled strings/choir) running the chord progression as sustained whole/half notes. Slow amp attack (~200-600 ms) and long release for smooth swells; lowpass with gentle resonance, often filter-automated to open during build-ups. Wide stereo (unison detune + chorus/ensemble), large hall reverb (long tail, high wet), optional shimmer/octave-up layer. In beat sections the pad is sidechained to the kick for the off-beat pulsing/ducking effect; in the breakdown the sidechain is removed so it sustains fully.

drums.machine: Layered modern EDM kit (not a single classic box): punchy synthesized/sampled trance kick (deep, tuned to track key, tight) + layered clap/snare (two claps + a snare layered for body) + crisp 16th hi-hats (closed) + open hats on the offbeat + ride in peak sections + occasional toms/percussion fills. Spiritually descended from TR-909-style four-on-floor but heavily processed/layered.
drums.kick: Four-on-the-floor: a kick on every quarter note (steps 1, 5, 9, 13). Deep, punchy, tuned to the track's root key, with a tight tail so it leaves room for the sub. The downbeat kicks are what the rolling bass ducks under via sidechain.
drums.snare: Layered clap+snare on the backbeat — beats 2 and 4 (steps 5 and 13). Two claps plus a snare layered together for a fuller hit, drenched in reverb in breakdown/build sections. Build-ups add accelerating snare/clap rolls (8th→16th→32nd) leading into the drop.
drums.hats: Closed hi-hats run a continuous stream of 16th notes (all 16 steps) for the shimmering drive, with velocity accents on the offbeats; OPEN hi-hats sit on the offbeat 8ths — steps 3, 7, 11, 15 (the 'and' of each beat, between the kicks). In peak/drop sections a ride cymbal reinforces those same offbeats for extra lift.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16 steps = one bar of 16th notes (beats on 1,5,9,13). KICK: 1,5,9,13 (four-on-floor). CLAP/SNARE: 5,13 (backbeat, beats 2 & 4). CLOSED HAT: every step 1-16 (16th stream, offbeats accented). OPEN HAT: 3,7,11,15 (offbeat 8ths, between kicks). RIDE (peak only): 3,7,11,15 doubling the open hats. ROLLING BASS: plays the offbeat 16ths — i.e. NOT on 1,5,9,13 (those are omitted under the kick) but on most of 2,3,4,6,7,8,10,11,12,14,15,16, with the note just before each beat jumping up an octave (classic gallop). Toms/percussion fills added at bar/phrase ends (e.g. steps 14-16) for variation.
drums.other: Risers/white-noise sweeps and downlifters for transitions; reverse cymbals into downbeats; impacts/crashes on phrase starts (step 1 of an 8/16/32-bar boundary); snare/clap rolls for build-ups; subtle shaker/conga/tom syncopation for groove. Crash cymbal lands on the '1' of each major section change.

signatureSounds: Supersaw anthem lead — 7 detuned saws (JP-8000 Super Saw lineage), wide unison, harmonic-minor melody doubled at octave + 3rd, big reverb/delay, filter-env opening on the drop
Rolling 16th offbeat-octave bassline — note on every 16th except under the kick, offbeats octave-jumped, mandatory sidechain duck to the kick
Four-on-the-floor trance kick — deep, tuned to key, tight tail, with continuous 16th closed-hats and offbeat open-hats
Lush detuned-saw string/choir pad ensemble — sus2/sus4/add9 voicings, slow attack, huge reverb, carrying the breakdown emotion
Sidechain 'pump' — kick-triggered ducking of bass and pads creating the off-beat pulsing groove
Build-up FX kit — accelerating snare/clap roll, rising white-noise sweep, pitch riser, reverse cymbal into the drop
Optional Roland 'Hoover'/acid (TB-303-style) accent stabs and arpeggiated saw plucks as secondary hooks
identityMustHaves: Four-on-the-floor kick at ~138 BPM with a sidechained rolling 16th offbeat-octave bassline locked to it
A wide detuned supersaw anthem lead playing a harmonic-minor (or major-lift) melody as the euphoric peak
An extended beatless breakdown (pads/strings + lead melody, big reverb) followed by a build-up (riser + snare roll) into the drop
Slow harmonic rhythm (1 chord per 1-2 bars) over an 8/16-bar emotive minor/major progression, e.g. i-VI-III-VII or vi-IV-I-V
engineRequirements: Supersaw synth voice: 7+ detuned saw oscillators with adjustable unison count, detune-spread in cents, and stereo width
Per-voice filter envelope with slow attack/sweep (filter-env opening the lead on the drop) AND tempo-synced filter automation for build-ups
Sidechain/ducking engine: kick-triggered envelope (adjustable attack/release/depth) applied to bass and pad busses — core to the genre groove and an expressive parameter
Octave-jump bass sequencing: per-16th-step octave offset so the engine can place the offbeat note an octave up while omitting the on-kick step
Multi-layer bass with independent EQ/filter per layer (sub + rolling-mid + hi/reese) summed and sidechained together
Harmony/voice-leading helper: generate octave doubling + 3rd/6th harmony layers of the lead from a chosen scale (harmonic minor with raised 7th)
Arpeggiator that subdivides slow chords into 16th-note patterns following a scale/chord
16th-note hi-hat stream with per-step velocity accenting (offbeat emphasis) and separate open-hat offbeat lane

Arrangement/automation engine able to mute/unmute whole busses for breakdown (beat+bass out) and stage build-ups (rising noise sweep, accelerating snare roll 8th→16th→32nd, filter sweep, riser, crash on the '1')
Tempo-synced FX: ping-pong/dotted delay and large reverb on lead/pads, plus white-noise riser + reverse-cymbal transition generators
Pad voice with slow attack/long release, ensemble detune, and sus2/sus4/add9 voicing support for lush chords
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible and well-documented — uplifting trance is one of the most formulaic, recipe-driven EDM genres, which is ideal for algorithmic generation. The structural skeleton (138 BPM, four-on-floor on steps 1/5/9/13, offbeat open-hats on 3/7/11/15, 16th hat stream, offbeat-octave sidechained rolling bass, i-VI-III-VII over 1-chord-per-bar) is deterministic and easy to encode. The two genuinely hard parts for a convincing result are (1) the supersaw timbre — getting the 7-voice detune/unison/filter-env/reverb stack to sound 'big' rather than thin requires real unison and stereo-width DSP, and (2) the macro-arrangement believability — the beatless breakdown → riser/snare-roll build → drop dynamic is what makes it 'feel' like trance, and that demands a bus-level mute/automation engine, not just per-track step data. A simple step sequencer can nail the groove loop but will sound flat without sidechain ducking, filter-envelope sweeps on the lead, and the breakdown/build/drop staging. Sources are production-community consensus (FL/Sylenth/JP-8000 tutorials, KVR forums, Splice/EDMProd guides) plus Wikipedia and a music-theory reference; specifics like the exact open-hat steps and the 'omit-the-on-kick-16th, octave-up-the-offbeat' bass rule are corroborated across multiple independent tutorials.

meter: 4/4 half-time (140 BPM grid, felt as ~70 BPM)
feel: Half-time, sparse, with syncopation/shuffle. Snare lands on beat 3 (not 2 and 4), so a 140 BPM track is internalized as ~70 BPM. Listeners "fill in" the implied double-time, creating tension. Optional ~50% swing on hats/shaker; tuplet/triplet fills allowed.

chordTypes: power chords (root+5th, often the bassline itself, no 3rd)
min7 and sus2/sus4 for melodic/'brostep'-adjacent leads
single drone/pedal root note (most of the tune sits on one root)
typicalProgressions: i (static drone — most classic dubstep loops on one minor/Phrygian root)
i - VI - III - VII (e.g. Am - F - C - G) for melodic/emotional dubstep
i - bII (Phrygian half-step move, e.g. Am - Bb) for menace
i - iv (e.g. Am - Dm)
tritone-built riffs: root against b5 within the bass riff for dissonance
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Very slow / mostly static. Classic dubstep drones on the tonic for whole 8-16 bar sections; harmony is harmonically simple with little modulation. Melodic/festival variants change chord every 1-2 bars.

form: 2-bar drum loop is the building block (bar 2 = a variation/fill of bar 1). Track architecture: intro -> build/riser -> DROP 1 (main section, the bass-drop) -> midsection/breakdown -> build -> DROP 2 (second main section, similar to drop 1) -> outro. Drops conventionally land after the 16th or 32nd bar. Sections are 8/16/32 bars. The defining dynamic event is the DROP: a beat-and-build emptiness resolving into the sub+wobble bassline on the downbeat. Heavy use of silence/space; arrangements are minimal.

bass.character: TWO co-existing bass roles. (1) SUB: a clean sine fundamental in 20-80 Hz carrying the root note, mono, no modulation, this is the body you feel. (2) WOBBLE/'wub' mid-bass: a gritty, rhythmically pulsing growl from ~80 Hz up that sits on top of the sub. Often glued/sidechained so they don't clash. The wobble is THE identity of the genre.
bass.signatureTechnique: LFO-modulated filter cutoff (the 'wub'/'wobble'). Serum-style recipe: saw wavetable, OSC octave -1, unison 2-4 voices @ ~0.10-0.15 detune, MG Low 24 (24 dB/oct lowpass) cutoff ~20% baseline, LFO (sine or rise/fall shape) tempo-synced routed to cutoff so it sweeps from nearly closed to ~70% open. Rate is performed/automated between 1/4, 1/8, 1/2, 1/16 (and triplet/dotted) per bar for the rhythmic growl. Add Tube distortion (~30% drive) for grit; reverb 10-15% mix, short decay. Sub = separate sine layer one octave below, unmodulated. Pitch and note follow root of key (often a single droned root). Bass riff is rhythmically syncopated, leaving holes in the half-time grid.
bass.synthesis: Subtractive virtual-analog + heavy LFO modulation. OSC: saw/square wavetable for wobble layer; pure sine for sub layer. Filter: resonant 24 dB/oct lowpass as the modulation target. Modulation: tempo-synced LFO -> filter cutoff (primary), optionally LFO -> volume/distortion/wavetable-position for variety. FX chain: waveshaper/tube distortion -> EQ (split sub vs mids) -> light reverb. Reese variant = 2+ detuned saws beating against each other for a wider, growling neuro-flavored bass.

lead.character: Sparse, dark, often dissonant. In classic dubstep the 'lead' is frequently just the bass riff itself or a detuned stab/pluck. In melodic dubstep, supersaw or pluck leads carry emotional minor melodies. Tritone and b2 (Phrygian) intervals used for menace.
lead.synthesis: Supersaw lead: 5-7 detuned saw oscillators, ~12-25 cent spread, lowpass with a slow filter-envelope opening, big hall reverb + 1/4 or 1/8-dotted delay. Pluck lead: single saw/square through a fast-decay amp/filter envelope (short attack, ~150-300 ms decay) for a stabby tone, often with unison detune and chorus. Detuned/atonal stabs run through bitcrush/distortion for grime-leaning tracks.

pads.character: Dark, atmospheric, reverberant. Used in intros/breakdowns to set mood and hold the minor/Phrygian root; pulled out or filtered down during the drop so the bass owns the space. Often dissonant or droning.
pads.synthesis: Supersaw pad: 7 detuned saw oscillators (~12-25 cent spread) through a 24 dB lowpass with a slow filter-envelope open, drenched in large hall/plate reverb (long decay) and a slow chorus. Often a low-pass filter sweep automated during the build for tension into the drop. Alternative: detuned warm analog-style triangle/saw pad, heavy reverb, sidechained gently to the kick.

drums.machine: Hybrid: punchy electronic kick + heavy electronic snare layered with a high-passed clap, plus syncopated electronic hats and shaker. Sample-based/909-adjacent rather than a single drum machine; often layered acoustic-snare/dub one-shots for character.
drums.kick: Tight, punchy, sub-reinforced kick on beat 1 (and a syncopated offbeat ghost/second kick before beat 3 for groove). Felt at the ~70 BPM half-time rate. EQ'd to leave room for the sub bass.
drums.snare: THE defining placement: heavy snare on beat 3 only (step 9 of 16), NOT on 2 and 4. Layered = weighty electronic snare emphasizing low-mids (~160-200 Hz) + reverb-treated, high-passed clap for the top. Big reverb tail. This single backbeat-displaced snare creates the half-time feel.
drums.hats: Closed hats / shaker drive the 140 BPM groove and syncopation, with subtle velocity shifts and ghost notes; they pause momentarily around beat 3 to give the snare space. Open hat used as an occasional offbeat accent. Occasional fast 1/32 stutters/rolls give the 'rushing' double-time feel.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step grid = one bar at 140 BPM (steps 1-16; beats land on 1,5,9,13). KICK: step 1 (downbeat), plus a syncopated ghost kick on step 7 or 8 (just before the snare) — classic minimal version is kick only on 1 and ~7. SNARE+CLAP: step 9 ONLY (beat 3, the half-time backbeat). CLOSED HATS: steps 3,5,7,11,13,15 (offbeat-leaning, with a deliberate gap around step 9) — or straight 1/8 on 3,7,11,15; add ghost hats on 'e/a' subdivisions with low velocity. OPEN HAT: occasional accent on step 15 (the 'and' of beat 4). EXTRA PERC/SHAKER: fills steps 2,6,10,14 lightly. In a 2-bar loop, bar 2 adds a fill (e.g. snare flam or 1/32 hat roll on steps 14-16) leading back to the downbeat.
drums.other: Reverberant dub one-shots, vinyl crackle/noise sweeps, impacts and risers into the drop, and a downlifter/sub-boom on the drop downbeat. Reverb tails and delay throws on snare are characteristic (the 'dub' heritage).

signatureSounds: The wobble / 'wub' bass: tempo-synced LFO sweeping a resonant lowpass cutoff on a detuned saw, layered over a clean sine sub (THE sound of dubstep)
Clean sine sub-bass at 20-80 Hz carrying the root note
Half-time snare on beat 3 with big reverb tail + high-passed clap layer
The DROP: build-up tension (riser, filter sweep, silence) resolving onto the sub+wobble on a downbeat
Reese bass: detuned beating saws for a wide growling bass (neuro/brostep flavor)
Supersaw chords/pads (7 detuned saws, lowpass with slow filter-env open, huge reverb) in melodic-dubstep intros/breakdowns
Risers/downlifters, impacts, and reverb/delay 'dub' throws as transition FX
Tritone and Phrygian b2 dissonance baked into the bass riff
identityMustHaves: Half-time feel at ~140 BPM with the snare on beat 3 (felt as ~70 BPM)
LFO-modulated wobble bass ('the wub') layered over a clean sine sub-bass
A DROP: tension/space resolving onto the bassline, with sparse, minimal arrangement and lots of empty space
Dark minor/Phrygian tonality, mostly droning on one root with little harmonic movement
engineRequirements: Half-time drum grid: snare locked to beat 3 (step 9), not 2 and 4 — the sequencer must support this displaced backbeat as a first-class feel

Tempo-synced LFO routable to filter cutoff (and to volume/distortion/wavetable-position) with per-bar selectable rate (1/4, 1/8, 1/2, 1/16, plus triplet/dotted) — this IS the wobble and is non-negotiable
Per-step or per-bar automation of LFO rate so the wobble pattern changes across the bar (performative 'wub-wub-WUBWUBWUB')
Two-layer bass voice: an unmodulated sine sub + a modulated mid-bass growl, with a crossover/EQ split so they coexist (and ideally mono-summed lows)
Resonant 24 dB/oct lowpass filter as a modulation target (the cutoff is the thing that wobbles)
Waveshaper/tube distortion stage on the bass for grit
Unison/detune (2-7 voices, cents spread) for Reese bass and supersaw leads/pads
Arrangement/section engine that can produce a DROP: build (riser + automated filter sweep + silence) -> downbeat hit, with sparse step programming and empty bars
Sidechain/ducking or layer-gating so sub and wobble don't clash and so pads duck under the drop
Reverb + tempo-synced delay sends (for the snare 'dub' throw and the cavernous half-time space)
Velocity/ghost-note support and 1/32 hat rolls/stutters for the 'rushing' double-time fills
Drone/single-root harmony mode (long static tonic) as well as a slow i-VI-III-VII style progression mode
feasibilityNote: Moderately-to-fairly hard to nail convincingly. The drum/harmony skeleton is easy (half-time grid, snare on 3, static minor/Phrygian root). The genre identity lives almost entirely in the bass: a believable wobble needs a tempo-synced LFO modulating a resonant lowpass cutoff (plus distortion) on a detuned-saw layer summed with a clean sine sub — a step-sequencer with only per-track volume/pitch cannot produce it. The single highest-value engine feature is the synced-LFO-to-cutoff with per-bar rate automation; without it you get a sub bassline but not 'dubstep.' Second-hardest is the DROP dynamic (build/silence -> downbeat resolution) which needs arrangement-level automation, not just looped patterns. Everything else (sparse programming, reverb throws, Reese/supersaw via unison detune) is standard subtractive synthesis. Sources are strong and consensual on tempo, the beat-3 snare, sub+wobble layering, and Phrygian/minor tonality.

meter: 4/4 (programmed on a 16th-note grid; drums perceived in double-time against a half-time harmonic/snare backbone)
feel: Rolling breakbeat with light swing/shuffle on the 16ths (~8-15% swing) and prominent ghost-snare syncopation; main backbeat sits on beat 3 (half-time-feel snare) while fast hat/ghost activity keeps the double-time roll. Laid-back, behind-the-beat, "liquid" smoothness.

chordTypes: dominant 7 used as a brightening surprise (e.g. ending on E major in A-minor)
typicalProgressions: Am9 - Cmaj7 - Em7 - Am9 - Cmaj7sus4/F - E (MusicRadar A-minor liquid example)
i9 - III maj7 - v7 - VII maj7 (Aeolian rolling vamp)
ii7 - V7 - Imaj7 (jazz 2-5-1 borrowed for soulful liquid)
i - VI - III - VII (Aeolian pop/anthemic)
Fm: i9 - iv9 vamp (F-minor is the most common production key for sub translation)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow and static for atmosphere: typically one chord per 1-2 bars over an 8-bar loop. Movement is created less by fast changes than by altering the top voice / adding incidental passing chords that nudge the upper notes while the bass holds.

form: Loop-based, 16-bar sections. Common arc: Intro/Breakdown (16) - Build (16, filter sweeps + reverse crash) - Drop 1 (16) - Drop 2 / variation (16) - Mid-breakdown (16) - Drop 3 (16) - Outro/Breakdown (16). Bass+drum phrases are 4 bars with a variation/fill every 8; a 2-beat silence ("breath") often punctuates phrase endings. The breakdown strips to chords/pads/vocal; the drop reintroduces the rolling break + sub. Builds use filter automation, reverse crashes, snare rolls and delay throws.

bass.character: Two-tier bass: (1) a deep, pure, warm SUB sine sitting at the fundamental (often F0/A0 range, key chosen so sub translates on club rigs); (2) a smoothed, slowly-evolving Reese providing mid-body and movement. In liquid the Reese is rounded/filtered rather than aggressive — growl tamed, sub emphasized. Bass is melodic, following the chord roots/3rds/5ths with a rolling 16th-note rhythm.
bass.signatureTechnique: Reese phasing: stack 2+ detuned saws (and/or unison 2 voices, mono, no stereo spread on the unison) so their phases beat against each other — the beating accelerates as pitch rises. Detune sweet spot ~0.10-0.20 (10-20 cents); beyond ~0.40 it splits into audible separate pitches. Separate the SUB onto its own clean sine layer (low-passed/mono, no detune) so the low end stays solid while the Reese carries movement. For liquid, low-pass the Reese to keep only some upper warmth, add slow filter/LFO motion and light saturation rather than heavy distortion.
bass.synthesis: Reese layer: 2x sawtooth oscillators, one fine-detuned up and one down (~±5-15 cents) for the core phase beat, OR unison count 2 with detune ~0.10-0.20, voicing MONO; low-pass filter (24dB) with cutoff kept fairly high then tamed to taste, slow filter-envelope/LFO for evolving movement, gentle drive/saturation. Sub layer: single sine (or triangle) one octave below, mono, low-passed, no detune, sidechained/ducked subtly under kick. Both layers note-locked to the chord harmony; glide/portamento optional between notes for the 'liquid' slide.

lead.character: Soulful and restrained rather than dominant — often a plucky Rhodes/electric-piano melody, a soft sine/triangle lead, a vibe-y bell/mallet, or chopped/pitched vocal phrases. Sits in the upper-mid, drenched in reverb/delay, with delay throws on phrase-ending notes.
lead.synthesis: Rhodes/EP via FM or sample for jazzy melody; or sine/triangle lead with a slow vibrato and big plate/hall reverb + 1/4 or 1/8-dotted delay throws. Soft attack, gentle filter, no harsh harmonics. Vocal leads: chopped, pitched, reverb-soaked one-shots used as melodic/textural hooks.

pads.character: Lush, warm, evolving and wide — the harmonic + emotional bed. Maj7/min9 voicings held under the break; the defining 'liquid' atmosphere. Often blended with soulful sampled piano/strings and ambient texture.
pads.synthesis: Supersaw/multi-saw stack (5-7 detuned saws, ~12-25 cent spread) and/or warm analog-style saw+triangle, low-pass with a slow filter-envelope opening, heavy hall/plate reverb (long tail), subtle chorus/ensemble for width, slow LFO on filter or amplitude for movement. Layer with sampled Rhodes/strings/choir and high-passed ambience. Sidechain lightly to the kick/snare so the break breathes through.

drums.machine: Chopped & resequenced acoustic breakbeat (Amen break and funk breaks the foundation), pitched up to 170-175 BPM, typically high-pass filtered and layered with punchy one-shot kick/snare (909/sampled) and a 'top loop' of high-end percussion. Two-step skeleton resequenced from the break.
drums.kick: Punchy layered one-shot on beat 1 (step 1) anchoring the bar; frequent second kick around the 'and' before beat 3 (steps ~7-8) or syncopated 16th placements borrowed from the Amen's displaced kicks. Tight, not boomy — sub is left to the bass.
drums.snare: Big main snare on beat 3 (step 11) for the half-time-feel backbone; in busier two-step a second main snare on beat 2/4 (steps 5/13). Quieter GHOST snares scattered on off-16ths (e.g. ~2.4 and 3.2 beat positions, steps ~6 and 10) at -6 to -12 dB add swing/roll. Snare often slightly behind the beat for laid-back feel.
drums.hats: Closed hats roughly on every 8th/16th filling the gaps, with humanized velocities for a live feel; open hat on offbeats for lift; pedalled/ghost hats low in the mix in early bars getting more prominent toward bar 4. Light shuffle on the 16ths.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16 steps = one 4/4 bar (steps 1-16, beats on 1/5/9/13). LIQUID TWO-STEP: Kick on step 1 (and a syncopated second kick on step 8). Main snare (backbeat) on step 11 (beat 3, half-time feel); add main snare on step 5 for the busier variant. Ghost snares (quiet, -8 to -12 dB) on steps 6 and 10 for the rolling shuffle. Closed hats on most 8th positions (1,3,5,7,9,11,13,15) with humanized velocity; open hat on offbeats step 3 and step 15. Across a 4-bar Amen-derived loop, displace the kick/snare in bars 3-4 (kick pulled to the start of bar 2, snare held back ~half a beat) to recreate the Amen groove rather than repeating bar 1 verbatim.
drums.other: Top loop / shaker / ride layer for high-end motion; reverse crash + reverse snare into drops; crash falling between beats 3 and 4 (Amen marker); snare rolls/edits as fills every 8 bars; rim/click ghosts. Subtle room reverb on the break, separate reverb send for drums.

signatureSounds: Pitched-up, chopped & resequenced acoustic breakbeat (Amen/funk break) — the rolling double-time engine
Two-layer bass: clean pure SUB sine + smoothed phasing Reese (2 detuned saws, ~10-20 cent detune, beating that accelerates with pitch)
Lush maj7/min9 pads (supersaw stack, slow filter open, long hall reverb) — the 'liquid' atmosphere
Soulful sampled Rhodes/electric-piano and reverb-soaked chopped vocal hooks
Extended jazz/soul chords (Am9, Cmaj7, Em7, Cmaj7sus4/F) voiced statically over a moving bass
Reverse crash / reverse snare swells and delay-throw transitions into the drop
identityMustHaves: 170-175 BPM rolling chopped breakbeat with ghost-snare shuffle and a half-time-feel main snare on beat 3
Deep clean sub-bass underpinning a smoothed/melodic (non-aggressive) Reese — warmth over distortion
Lush extended-jazz harmony (maj7/min7/min9 etc.) carried by pads + soulful piano/Rhodes, static harmonic rhythm
Musical, emotive, 'liquid' atmosphere (reverb-drenched, soulful, melodic) distinguishing it from neuro/jump-up DnB
engineRequirements: Breakbeat slicing & resequencing: load a break, slice to 16ths, re-trigger slices per step (not just one-shot triggers) with per-slice pitch/reverse/gate to recreate Amen-style displaced grooves
Per-step velocity + micro-timing/swing (8-15%) and per-track timing offset for behind-the-beat snares and humanized hats
Ghost-hit layer support: low-velocity ghost snares/hats as a distinct dynamic tier (-6 to -12 dB)
Reese monosynth: 2+ detuned saw oscillators OR unison with mono voicing and controllable detune (cents) producing phase-beating; plus glide/portamento
Independent dual-bass routing: a separate clean mono sub-sine layer locked an octave below the Reese, with its own low-pass and ducking
Tempo-synced filter LFO / filter-envelope automation for slow bass and pad movement (and build sweeps)
Supersaw/multi-oscillator pad stack (5-7 voices, adjustable detune spread) with slow amp/filter envelope
Polyphonic chord engine that voices extended jazz chords (7/9/11, sus, slash/inversions) and supports voice-leading / incidental top-note changes over a static bass
Send-effects architecture: separate reverb sends for drums / music / bass, plus tempo-synced delay 'throws' on lead and vocal
Sidechain/ducking of pads and sub to the kick & snare so the break breathes
Arrangement/automation lane for 16-bar section structure with reverse-crash risers and filter builds
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible to generate convincingly, with one hard dependency: the genre's identity lives in the CHOPPED breakbeat, so the engine needs real break-slicing/resequencing (or a very good multi-sample acoustic kit with ghost-hit dynamics and micro-timing) — a plain 4-on-the-floor step sequencer cannot fake the Amen roll. The dual-layer bass (clean sub sine + phasing Reese) and lush extended-jazz pads are straightforward subtractive-synth tasks. The trickiest musical element is the harmony: convincing liquid needs correct extended-chord voicings (maj7/min9/min11, slash chords) and tasteful static-yet-moving progressions — a random-note generator will sound wrong, so a key/scale-aware chord engine with curated progressions is required. Light swing, behind-the-beat snares, and reverb depth carry most of the 'liquid' smoothness. All numbers above are grounded in production-community sources (some detune figures are Serum/Vital-specific consensus values rather than universal constants).

feel: Straight 16ths with light shuffle/swing applied to hats and stabs (~52-60% swing); kick stays dead-straight while hats/percussion/bass carry the groove. Overall hypnotic and machine-tight rather than humanized.

scalesModes: Dorian (for the slightly brighter, funkier Detroit flavor)
chordTypes: minor 7th (m7 = root, b3, 5, b7 — 'pure Detroit' with wide voicing)
minor 9th (m9, for lush Carl Craig-style pads)
parallel/fixed-interval voicings (whole chord shape transposed in parallel, no diatonic re-voicing — the classic 'chord memory' sound)
typicalProgressions: i (single tonic chord vamped for 8-16+ bars — the most common 'progression' is essentially none; harmony evolves via filter/automation not chord change)
i - VI - VII - v (Aeolian loop, e.g. Cm - Ab - Bb - Gm)
i - iv (e.g. Cm - Fm, two-chord hypnotic see-saw)
Cm - Gm - Fm - Gm - Abm - Bbm (fixed-interval parallel-minor stab line — all chords keep identical minor-triad shape, just transposed)
i - bVII - i - v (Dorian/Aeolian vamp)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Extremely slow to static. Often ONE chord/mode held for an entire 8-32 bar phrase; when chords move, typically 1 chord per bar or per 2 bars. Harmonic interest comes from rhythmic stab placement and parameter automation (filter, resonance) rather than frequent chord changes. This is the defining 'hypnotic loop' trait.

form: Loop-and-evolve DJ-track structure, not verse/chorus. Loop length 4-8 bars; phrases grouped in 16/32-bar blocks. Long beat-mixable intro (16-32 bars of kick + hats + minimal perc for DJ blending), then layered additive arrangement: kick first, then hats/perc, then sub-bass, then stabs, then pad/lead. Breakdown(s) drop the kick to expose pads/stabs with rising filter/reverb tension, then a re-entry ('drop' = kick + bass return, often with sidechain pump). Outro mirrors intro (strip back to kick + hats) for mixing out. Total length typically 6-8 minutes. Evolution is gradual and subtle: every 8-16 bars introduce/remove one element or nudge a filter, rather than dramatic EDM-style drops.

bass.character: Deep, round, monophonic sub/analog bass locked tightly to the kick — repetitive and hypnotic. Two canonical modes: (1) a single-note or root-fifth offbeat 'pulse' sitting in the gaps between kicks, or (2) a rolling 16th-note line where offbeat notes drop an octave for syncopated drive. Often follows or implies the root of the held minor chord.
bass.signatureTechnique: Offbeat placement so bass never collides with the kick fundamental, plus sidechain compression keyed off the kick (bass ducks on each kick hit and swells back — the 'pump'). Slight glide/portamento between notes for the classic SH-101 slide. Accent on offbeats; root-with-octave-jumps for the rolling variant.
bass.synthesis: Monosynth, 1-2 oscillators, sawtooth or square (square/PWM for hollow Reese-ish weight, saw for fuller body), optional sub-oscillator one octave down. 24dB/oct lowpass filter with cutoff kept low (emphasize fundamental + low harmonics), modest resonance, short-to-medium amp decay so notes are punchy and gated. Add light glide. Model: Roland SH-101 / TB-303-adjacent / Juno bass. Heavy sidechain to kick.

lead.character: Sparse, hypnotic motif rather than a vocal-style melody. Often an arpeggio or short repeating cell (3-6 notes) from the underlying minor scale, in a higher octave, with notes drawn from the held chord (9ths, 7ths, 11ths in a tight cluster on top). Delay/echo trails create the 'rolling' Detroit shimmer (e.g. dotted-8th delay). Frequently a single 'bleep' tone (Atkins/Mills 'bleep techno') or a slowly transposing fixed-interval cell.
lead.synthesis: Mono or paraphonic synth, saw or pulse oscillator, resonant 24dB lowpass with an envelope/LFO sweeping the cutoff for movement; short pluck-style amp envelope. Run through tempo-synced delay (dotted-8th or 1/8) with feedback and into reverb. Classic sources: Roland SH-101, Juno-106, Yamaha DX (FM bell/bleep), Korg MS-style. Pitch automation/transposition over the loop for evolution.

pads.character: Lush, warm, wide minor 7th/9th sustained chords providing the emotional/'soulful' Detroit core — the 'strings' or 'space chords'. Wide voicing (spanning ~2 octaves, mostly 5ths and octaves in the body) with the 7th/9th color on top. Slow swelling, often filtered down and opening across the phrase. Sits behind/around the stabs; in breakdowns it becomes the lead emotional element.
pads.synthesis: Polyphonic analog-style synth, 2+ detuned sawtooth oscillators per voice (or PWM with LFO-modulated pulse width for the 'deep Juno strings'), lush analog chorus (Juno-106 chorus II is the archetype), slow filter attack so the chord blooms, long release, big plate/hall reverb. PWM + sub-osc + heavy chorus is the canonical Juno-string recipe. Models: Juno-106, Jupiter-8, SH-style. Slow LFO on filter/pitch for drift.

drums.machine: Roland TR-909 is the defining kit (kick, clap, hats, ride). Often layered/dirtied via 12-bit sampling (MPC60/SP-1200) for grit. TR-808 sometimes layered for deeper sub-kick and cowbell/clave; TR-707 for extra claps. Kick frequently distorted/saturated.
drums.kick: Punchy 909 kick on every quarter note (four-on-the-floor), dead straight, no variation. Tuned deep/bass-heavy, with a tight click transient; often distorted/saturated and used as the sidechain trigger for bass.
drums.snare: Clap (909/707) is the backbeat element rather than a true snare, on beats 2 and 4 (steps 5 and 13). Optional layered rimshot/snare pitched down a couple semitones for darker accent, deliberately avoiding landing on a kick step. In sparser minimal passages the clap may drop out, leaving only kick + hats.
drums.hats: Closed 909 hats drive the groove: steady 8ths or 16ths, with the OPEN hat on every offbeat (the 'and' of each beat) for the signature pumping push-pull. Two layered closed-hat sounds common — one sharp/bright, one softer/longer — alternated for movement. Open hat is choked by the next closed hat. Light swing (52-60%) on hats; subtle velocity/accent variation for the 'rolling' Jeff Mills feel.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step grid (1 bar of 16th notes). KICK: steps 1,5,9,13 (four-on-floor, accented). CLAP/SNARE: steps 5,13 (backbeat on 2 and 4). CLOSED HAT: 8th-note version on steps 1,3,5,7,9,11,13,15 (or full 16ths on every step for busier rolling feel), with accents on steps 4,8,12,16. OPEN HAT: steps 3,7,11,15 (every offbeat, choked by following closed hat). RIDE (Jeff Mills rolling option): doubles the open-hat offbeats 3,7,11,15 at -6dB. RIM/PERC: syncopated, e.g. steps 4,12 or 7,15 — never on a kick step. Velocity/automation evolves across the loop rather than the step placement changing.
drums.other: Ride cymbal layered on offbeats for 'rolling' tracks (Jeff Mills); occasional 909 crash on the downbeat of a new 16/32-bar phrase. 808 cowbell/clave, shaker, or congas for minimal-techno syncopation. A single tom or rimshot fill marks phrase turnarounds. Subtle bursts of additional 16th-note hats/percussion ('rolls') signal builds. Heavy sidechain pump from kick into bass/pads is itself a rhythmic element.

signatureSounds: TR-909 kit — straight four-on-floor kick, offbeat open hats, clap on 2&4 — the rhythmic backbone (909 kick distorted/saturated)

Detroit 'space chord' STAB: short, punchy minor7/min9 chord stab, parallel/fixed-interval voicing, played on offbeats with a tight pluck envelope, soaked in tempo-synced delay + reverb — typically a detuned 2-3 oscillator saw synth (Juno/SH) into a resonant lowpass
Fixed-interval / 'chord-memory' parallel-minor motif: one chord shape transposed up/down the scale without re-voicing (e.g. Cm-Gm-Fm-Abm-Bbm), giving the un-diatonic, machine-like Detroit melody
Lush Juno-106 chorus strings/pad on a wide min7/min9 voicing (PWM + sub + chorus II) for the 'soulful' emotional layer
Rolling offbeat sub-bass locked to kick with glide + sidechain pump (SH-101/303-style mono)
FM/analog 'bleep' lead tone with dotted-8th delay (bleep-techno lineage)
Sidechain 'pump' — kick ducking the bass/pads — as an audible rhythmic signature
Subtle, continuous filter-cutoff and resonance automation creating motion within a static loop
identityMustHaves: Four-on-the-floor 909 kick at ~125-135 BPM with offbeat open hats — the non-negotiable rhythmic skeleton
Static/near-static minor harmony (one mode or a 2-chord vamp held for many bars) — the 'hypnotic loop': interest comes from repetition + subtle evolution, not chord changes
The Detroit chord STAB — short minor7/min9 stab, ideally parallel/fixed-interval, off-beat placement, with delay/reverb
Subtle continuous evolution via parameter automation (filter cutoff/resonance, send levels, additive layering) rather than dramatic arrangement events — 'loops that breathe'
engineRequirements: Step sequencer at 16th-note resolution with per-step velocity/accent AND adjustable swing/shuffle (~52-60%) applied selectively to hats/perc but NOT the kick
909-style drum voices with open-hat choke (closed hat cuts off open hat) and accent/total-accent control
Sidechain ducking: kick must be routable as a trigger to duck bass and pads (the 'pump') — core to the groove, not just mixing
Monosynth with portamento/glide + accent for the rolling bass and bleep lead (303/SH-101 behavior)

Per-note/per-loop parameter AUTOMATION lanes (filter cutoff, resonance, send/reverb, volume) that evolve over 8-32 bars — the single most important capability for 'subtle evolution'; without it the loop is static and lifeless
Tempo-synced delay (esp. dotted-8th and 1/8) and reverb sends on stabs/leads/pads
Parallel / fixed-interval chord voicing: ability to define a chord SHAPE and transpose it as a rigid block (no diatonic re-voicing) to reproduce the 'chord-memory' Detroit sound
Polyphonic pad voice with detuned-saw or PWM oscillators + chorus + slow filter-attack envelope (Juno-strings emulation)

Additive/subtractive arrangement engine: mute/unmute and introduce layers on 8/16/32-bar boundaries, with build/fill triggers (extra 16th hat rolls, crash on phrase downbeat) and breakdown logic (drop kick, raise filter/reverb, re-enter)
Arpeggiator (tempo-synced) for the higher-octave melodic cell
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible for a step-sequencer + subtractive-synth engine — this genre is arguably the easiest electronic style to generate convincingly because its identity lives in a rigid, well-documented drum grid (909 four-on-floor + offbeat open hats), near-static minor harmony, and a small set of synth recipes. The hard part is NOT the notes but the EVOLUTION: a literal static loop sounds like a demo, not a track. Convincing output requires (a) continuous, slow parameter automation (filter sweeps, send swells, subtle per-bar variation) and (b) additive 8/16/32-bar arrangement with breakdowns/builds — both of which are engine features beyond per-track params. Sidechain pump and open-hat choke are also load-bearing for the 'feel.' The fixed-interval/parallel chord stab is the one harmonic subtlety that gives away authenticity and is trivial to implement (transpose a chord block) once recognized. Get the 909 grid, the offbeat sub-bass + sidechain, one min7 parallel stab with delay, and slow filter automation, and the result reads unmistakably as Detroit/minimal techno. Tempo note: pure Detroit skews faster (128-140, typ. 135), pure minimal slightly slower (122-130, typ. 128-130); the combined target sits at ~125-132, so 128 is the safe typical.

meter: 4/4 half-time (notated/sequenced at ~140 BPM but felt at ~70 BPM)
feel: Half-time with 2-step garage shuffle/swing on the 16th-note grid (swing ~55-65%); hats and ghost percussion are humanized off-grid so the groove glides rather than marches. Underlying pulse is sparse and spacious, never four-on-the-floor driving.

scalesModes: Dorian (for a less dark, soulful color)
Lydian flavoring over the IV/major chords via add9/#11 extensions
typicalProgressions: i-VI-III-VII (e.g. Fm7 - Dbmaj7 - Abmaj7 - Ebmaj7 in F minor)
Fm7 - Ebmaj7(sus2) - Bbmaj(add9) - Dbmaj7 (NI chillstep tutorial progression)
i - iv - VI - v (all sevenths/ninths, e.g. Fm9 - Bbm7 - Dbmaj7 - Cm7)
vi - IV - I - V reharmonized with 7ths/9ths for the brighter/uplifting chillstep variant
i - VII - VI sustained drone-style loop for atmospheric sections
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow: typically 1 chord per bar or 1 chord per 2 bars; pads sustain and overlap (legato/voice-stealing) so changes feel like gentle swells rather than rhythmic stabs. 4-chord loops are the norm.

form: Intro/atmosphere (pads + field recording, no drums) -> first drop/body where half-time drums + sub enter -> breakdown (drums drop out, pads/vocal chop swell with reverb tail) -> second body, often fuller. Loops are typically 4 or 8 bars; full arrangement 8-16 bars repeating with additive layering. There is a 'drop' but it is an emotional swell / bass-and-drum entrance, NOT an aggressive bass design moment as in dubstep. Long reverb tails and risers bridge sections.

bass.character: Deep, warm, legible sub-bass that sits low and round; in Future Garage flavor often a Reese (two detuned saws beating against each other through a lowpass) for harmonic movement, while Chillstep leans toward a cleaner sine/triangle sub with subtle waveform-LFO motion. Throbbing legato lines that glide between root notes following the chord roots.
bass.signatureTechnique: Legato/portamento note transitions following chord roots, one note per chord or simple syncopated root figure; sidechain-ducked gently under the kick; sub fundamental kept mono and below ~120 Hz with a clean low-pass. Reese variant: detune beating + slow filter LFO for evolving timbre.
bass.synthesis: Chillstep sub: 1-2 sine/triangle oscillators transposed down ~-2 octaves, lowpass with resonance, slow custom LFO modulating the wavetable/waveform for movement (Serum-style), light saturation for audibility on small speakers. Reese (Future Garage): 2 saw oscillators detuned +0.30/-0.30 semitones (~30 cents each way), MONO voicing, lowpass cutoff ~650 Hz, resonance ~14%, optional sweeping notch/filter LFO + drive/overdrive; add a clean sub oscillator beneath the two detuned saws for solid low end.

lead.character: Restrained and emotive rather than virtuosic: simple, vocal-like melodic phrases with lots of space. Often a pitched/chopped vocal fragment, an electric-piano lead, a soft plucked synth, or a sine/triangle counter-melody. Heavily reverbed and delayed so it blends into the atmosphere as texture more than a hook.
lead.synthesis: Electric piano (FM/Rhodes-style, e.g. 'Smoking Phoenix' with low preamp drive ~23%) for warm leads; OR pitched vocal one-shots ('helium' re-pitched, formant-shifted) run through quarter-note delay + large dark reverb; OR a soft sine/triangle synth lead kept low in the mix (~-20 dB relative) for subtle counter-melody. Always wide stereo, low velocity, soft attack.

pads.character: Lush, warm, foggy and evolving — the emotional core of the genre. Wide stereo, slow attack and release, gently moving filter and chorus so the chord 'breathes'. Dark and nocturnal, sitting in a big hall/cathedral reverb. Often layered (one bright airy pad + one warm low pad).
pads.synthesis: Supersaw/multisaw pad: 5-7 detuned saw oscillators (~12-25 cent unison spread) into a lowpass filter with a slow filter-envelope/LFO that opens over 1-2 bars; wide unison stereo spread, chorus, and a large dark reverb (e.g. Raum 'Large > Dark Mod Hall', long decay). Layer with FM/granular or wavetable pads (Reaktor Prism, FM8, granular textures) filtered and spread wide. Add subtle pitch/filter LFO motion, high-cut to keep it warm, and send generously to reverb + a 1/2-note-synced delay (low feedback) so tails overlap into a wash.

drums.machine: Hybrid: layered acoustic/electronic one-shots. Kick often a layered round 808/707/909-style sub-kick blended with a clicky transient; snare/clap layered with a reverbed rimshot; crisp short closed hats; tight open hats; plus organic percussion (shaker, tambourine, rimshot, vinyl crackle, field-recording FX). Not a single drum machine — sample-collage in the 2-step/garage tradition.
drums.kick: Half-time placement: strong kick on step 1, second kick syncopated around the '+ of 3' (step ~11), i.e. the classic 2-step 'first beat + 8th-note between beats 3 and 4'. Velocity-varied, one kick often nudged off-grid with manual swing. Layered for rounded low-end + slight high-end click.
drums.snare: Backbeat snare/clap on step 9 (the '3' = the half-time backbeat), often layered snare + reverbed rimshot. In busier 2-step bars an additional ghost/secondary snare can fall near step 13. Big, reverbed, sits back in the mix.
drums.hats: Swung 16th closed hats on offbeats with short decay; an 'in-between' open/half-open hat on the offbeats (steps 3,7,11,15) and ghost hats off-grid for the rolling skip. Hats are velocity-varied (e.g. accents ~68, ghosts ~30) and humanized late/early to create the garage shuffle. Swing 55-65%.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step grid, ONE bar at ~140 BPM felt half-time (backbeat on step 9): KICK on step 1 and step 11 (the syncopated '+ of 3', often nudged off-grid by manual swing). SNARE/CLAP+rimshot on step 9 (the half-time backbeat), optional ghost snare ~step 13. CLOSED HATS swung on offbeats 3,7,11,15 with extra ghost hats and short decay; OPEN/half-open HAT on 7 and 15. SHAKER/tambourine rolling across many 16ths at low velocity for the 2-step 'skip'. VINYL CRACKLE + atmospheric FX layer underneath throughout. Apply 55-65% 16th-note swing and humanize hats/perc off the grid.
drums.other: Vinyl crackle loop and field recordings (rain, street noise) run continuously as texture; reverbed 'big perc' hit to fill sparse beats; subtle rising/falling noise FX and reversed cymbals between sections. Gentle bus compression (attack ~10 ms) to glue without squashing; drum bus pulled back ~-6 dB for headroom under the pads.

signatureSounds: Lush evolving supersaw/wavetable pad (5-7 detuned saws, slow filter-open, huge dark reverb) — the emotional anchor
Warm legible sub-bass (sine/triangle, legato glide) OR detuned-saw Reese bass (~30c detune, lowpass ~650 Hz, beating) for the Future Garage flavor
Pitched/chopped vocal fragments ('helium' re-pitched, formant-shifted) used as texture/counter-melody, drenched in delay + reverb
Shuffled half-time 2-step drums: syncopated ghost kick, reverbed backbeat snare/rimshot, short swung skippy hats
Vinyl crackle + field recordings (rain, street, noise) for a nocturnal lo-fi atmosphere
Warm electric-piano / Rhodes-style chords and leads (FM, low drive)
Long 1/2-note-synced delays and large dark hall/cathedral reverbs that smear everything into an ambient wash
identityMustHaves: Half-time feel at ~130-145 BPM (drums felt at ~70 BPM) with a 2-step garage shuffle/swing — never four-on-the-floor
Deep, clean, present sub-bass as the harmonic floor
Lush, emotive, reverb-drenched evolving pads on slow minor-key 7th/9th progressions
Atmospheric depth: vinyl crackle / field recordings / big dark reverb tails creating a nocturnal, melancholic mood
engineRequirements: Half-time drum grid (backbeat on step 9 of 16) decoupled from the felt tempo
Per-step swing 55-65% on the 16th grid PLUS per-hit micro-timing humanization (late/early offsets) for the garage shuffle
Per-step velocity variation (accents vs ghost notes) on hats and percussion
Layered multi-sample drum voices (kick = sub layer + click; snare = body + reverbed rimshot)
Supersaw/unison engine: 5-7 detuned oscillators with adjustable cent-spread + stereo unison
Per-voice slow filter envelope/LFO to open pads over 1-2 bars (modulation routing beyond static cutoff)
Monophonic bass voice with portamento/legato glide between notes
Reese capability: two saws with fine cent detune + filter LFO/sweeping notch
Polyphonic pad voices with slow attack/release and voice-stealing/legato chord overlaps
Tempo-synced send effects: large reverb (long decay) + 1/2-note delay with feedback control as bus sends
Sidechain/ducking of bass and pads under the kick
Continuous texture layer (loop a vinyl-crackle/field-recording sample under the whole arrangement)
Pitch/formant-shifting of vocal one-shots for 'helium' chops
Chord/voice-leading engine that builds min7/maj7/add9/sus extensions and moves them slowly (1 chord per bar)
feasibilityNote: Moderately achievable and a good fit for a generative engine, because the genre is forgiving: sparse arrangements, slow harmonic rhythm, and atmosphere-over-precision mean small timing/voicing imperfections read as 'vibe' rather than errors. The hardest parts to nail convincingly are (1) the 2-step shuffle 'glide' — it needs real per-hit micro-timing + velocity humanization, not just a global swing knob; (2) lush evolving pads — requires a supersaw/unison stack with slow filter-envelope modulation and generous tempo-synced reverb/delay sends, otherwise pads sound static and thin; and (3) the emotive minor-key 7th/9th voice-leading, which benefits from a real chord engine rather than random notes. Pitched vocal chops add huge authenticity but depend on a sample/formant-shift source. Without filter modulation, send reverbs, and micro-timing the result will sound like generic minor-key half-time rather than true Chillstep/Future Garage. Sub-bass and the half-time drum grid are trivial to implement. Sources consulted live; one (MasterClass) returned HTTP 403 and was substituted with Wikipedia + production-community consensus.

genre: Lo-fi Hip Hop (lofi hip-hop / chillhop / "study beats")
feel: Swung boom-bap with a loose, "drunk"/off-grid human feel. 8th-note swing of roughly 54-62% (the canonical "Dilla swing" sits ~53-56%); hats and percussion are swung while the kick/snare backbone stays near-grid but is deliberately NOT perfectly quantized — snares pushed slightly early, hats/kicks dragged slightly late so the groove "lopes." Velocities humanized; ghost notes and micro-timing drift are essential. Often feels half-time relative to the BPM count.

scalesModes: Harmonic minor (for the raised-7th V7 cadence into i)
typicalProgressions: ii7 - V7 - Imaj7 (classic jazz 2-5-1, e.g. Dm7 - G7 - Cmaj7)
Imaj7 - vi7 - ii7 - V7 (jazz turnaround, e.g. Cmaj7 - Am7 - Dm7 - G7)
i7 - iv7 (modal two-chord vamp, e.g. Am7 - Dm7, looped)
VImaj7 - i - V7 (e.g. in A minor: Fmaj7 - Am - E7, the Native Instruments example)
imin9 - III maj7 (Dorian color vamp)
i7 - VI7 - VII7 (descending minor)
Imaj7 - IVmaj7 (lydian-ish lift, e.g. Cmaj7 - Fmaj7)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow and loop-based: typically 1 chord per bar or 1 chord per 2 beats, over a short 2- or 4-bar loop that repeats for the whole track. Many tracks use just 2-4 chords. The loop's repetition is part of the aesthetic; harmonic motion is minimal and mood-driven, not developmental.

form: Loop-centric and minimal. A 2-, 4-, or 8-bar chord+drum loop is the entire song's foundation. Arrangement is additive/subtractive layering rather than verse/chorus: bring in one element at a time over 4- or 8-bar phrases (drums in, then keys, then bass, then texture), strip elements back when repetition fatigues, reintroduce them. Common skeleton: intro (vinyl crackle + keys, no drums) -> drop the drums -> full loop -> brief break (drop drums or filter-sweep down) -> return -> outro that filters/fades out. Tracks are short (often ~1.5-2.5 min, "study beat" length). Frequent spoken-word/movie/anime vocal samples or field recordings (rain, café, vinyl pops) bookend or punctuate sections.

bass.character: Warm, round, deep and uncomplicated — sits low and supports rather than leads. Often a soft sine/sub or a sampled/synth upright (double) bass with finger-noise and slight pitch drift. Plays mostly root notes (and occasional 5ths/octaves or walking passing tones in jazzier tracks), one note per chord, locked loosely to the kick. Low-passed heavily (filtered around/below ~150 Hz to concentrate low end) and frequently sidechained to the kick.
bass.signatureTechnique: Root-note-per-chord with a short, soft, slightly-late attack so it pumps under the kick; occasional walking bass (passing notes between chord roots) for jazz-hop feel; mono and centered; subtle wow/flutter pitch wobble and finger/fret noise for the 'upright sampled off a record' illusion.
bass.synthesis: Option A (sub): single sine oscillator, fundamental + very light saturation/2nd-harmonic for audibility on small speakers, fast-ish amp attack ~5-15 ms, gentle release, lowpass ~120-150 Hz. Option B (upright emulation): sampled/physical-model double bass or a triangle+sine layer with a noise-burst transient for the pluck, pitch-envelope dip, tape wobble LFO (~0.3-1 Hz, a few cents) on pitch, and a touch of room/plate reverb. Both: mono, sidechain-ducked by kick.

lead.character: Sparse, wistful, jazzy — usually short melodic phrases or arpeggiated chord fragments rather than busy leads. Most often a muted/processed electric piano (Rhodes) figure, a soft jazz/nylon guitar lick, a filtered sample melody, or a mellow synth. Frequently the 'lead' is just the chord instrument's top voice or a sampled melodic loop, low-passed and drenched in vinyl noise. Phrases leave space; the genre prizes restraint.
lead.synthesis: Rhodes/electric-piano model (tine FM-ish bell + filtered triangle body) OR sampled instrument. Heavy lowpass (gentle slope, cutoff ~2-3.12 kHz to remove crispy highs), tape/vinyl coloration, slight detune/chorus, generous spring/plate reverb and tape delay (often 1/4 or dotted-1/8 synced), and bitcrush/downsample (SP-1200/SP-303-style ~12-bit, reduced sample rate) for grit. Pitch wow/flutter for the 'warped tape' feel.

pads.character: Subtle background warmth and glue — soft, dark, slow-evolving. Provides harmonic bed and nostalgia, never foreground. Often a filtered Rhodes/string ensemble, a warm analog-style pad, mellotron-ish strings/flute, or a sustained sampled texture. Sits low in the mix under the keys, heavily low-passed and noisy.
pads.synthesis: 2-3 detuned saw/triangle oscillators (small detune, a few cents) OR sampled string/mellotron, lowpass with slow filter-envelope open (cutoff ~1-3 kHz, slow attack 0.5-2 s), long release, big plate/hall reverb, chorus/ensemble, and tape saturation + vinyl hiss/crackle layered on top. Volume automation/swells to breathe under the loop.

drums.machine: Boom-bap kit, typically sampled/processed acoustic hits run through (or emulated as) an Akai MPC (MPC60/3000/2000XL) or E-mu SP-1200/SP-303 — i.e. 12-bit, reduced-sample-rate 'dusty' drums. Often a chopped acoustic break layered with individual one-shots. Not 808/909 four-on-the-floor; the SP-1200/MPC crunch and swing engine are the identity.
