drums.kick: Soft, round, 'thumpy' low kick (not a sharp EDM kick). Lands on beat 1 and around beat 3, often with an extra syncopated/ghost kick just before the snare for the classic 'boom-...-boom-bap'. Slightly behind the beat; lowpassed, saturated, sometimes sidechain source for bass.
drums.snare: Loose, dusty, often a layered acoustic snare or rimshot/clap with vinyl crackle baked in. Lands on beats 2 and 4 (the boom-BAP backbeat). Pushed marginally early in Dilla-style grooves; sometimes doubled with a soft clap and decorated with quiet ghost snares between backbeats.
drums.hats: Closed hi-hats mostly on straight 8th notes (8 per bar) but SWUNG (~54-62%), with humanized velocity and occasional 16th-note rolls/stutters; an open hi-hat frequently on the last offbeat of the bar (the 'and' of 4) as a turnaround. Hats often played freehand/unquantized for the loose feel and low-passed to take off harshness.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16-step bar (steps 1-16; backbeats on 5 and 13). KICK: steps 1, 4 (or 7) and 11 — i.e. 1 = 'boom', a syncopated ghost/extra kick on the 'a' before the 2nd backbeat (step 11 leading into 13), giving boom-(boom)-bap; minimal and varied per bar. SNARE: steps 5 and 13 (beats 2 and 4) — the backbone — with optional soft ghost snare around step 8/16. CLOSED HAT: steps 1,3,5,7,9,11,13,15 (every 8th) but with swing delaying the even-numbered offbeat hats (3,7,11,15) by ~54-62%, plus occasional extra 16th hats (e.g. a quick step 15+16 roll). OPEN HAT: step 15 (the last offbeat, the 'and of 4') as a recurring turnaround accent. PERC/extras: light shaker or rimclick ghosting offbeats; vinyl crackle runs continuously underneath. Crucially, NOT every hit is on-grid — kick dragged late, snare nudged early, hats freehand.
drums.other: Continuous vinyl crackle/pop loop and tape hiss as a 'percussion' layer; soft shaker or tambourine on offbeats; finger snaps/claps doubling the snare; occasional foley/field recording (rain, café, page turns). All bus-compressed and tape-saturated together; drum bus low-passed (~2 kHz) so transients are soft and 'pushed back'.

signatureSounds: Dusty boom-bap drums sampled/downsampled through an MPC or SP-1200 (12-bit, lowered sample rate) — soft thumpy kick + loose backbeat snare with baked-in grit
Rhodes / electric-piano chords voiced as maj7/min9 jazz chords, low-passed, chorused and drenched in vinyl noise — the harmonic signature of the genre
Continuous vinyl crackle + surface noise + pop (e.g. iZotope Vinyl-style) plus tape hiss — the 'lo-fi' in lo-fi
Tape wow & flutter / pitch wobble on keys, samples and bass for the warped-cassette warmth
Heavy gentle low-pass filtering (cutoff ~2-3 kHz on melodics/drums, ~150 Hz on bus highs) that removes crispy top and 'pushes everything back' into a hazy mix
Bitcrush / sample-rate reduction (SP-1200/SP-303 emulation) for crunchy aliased grit on drums and samples
Sidechain pump (kick ducking the chords/bass), embraced overtly as part of the aesthetic
Warm sub or sampled upright bass on chord roots, mono and filtered low
Jazzy spoken/movie/anime vocal samples and field recordings (rain, café ambience) as texture
Soft, dark background pad/string bed for warmth and glue
identityMustHaves: Swung, deliberately-imperfect (un-perfectly-quantized) boom-bap drum groove at ~60-90 BPM with the 'kick boom / snare bap' backbone — the loose human/Dilla feel is non-negotiable
Jazzy extended-chord harmony (maj7/min7/9ths/11ths) on Rhodes/electric-piano or sampled keys, in short repeating loops
Lo-fi 'dust': vinyl crackle + tape hiss/saturation + gentle low-pass filtering removing the highs (without these it's just clean jazz-hop/instrumental hip hop, not lo-fi)
Minimal, mood-over-complexity arrangement — sparse layers, lots of space, loop-based
engineRequirements: Per-step micro-timing offsets (push/pull individual hits off the grid by ms or ticks) — required for the snare-early / kick-late / freehand-hat 'Dilla swing' feel
Adjustable swing amount on 8th (and 16th) notes, continuously variable ~50-65%, applied per-track (swing hats but not snare)
Per-step velocity/humanization with random jitter (timing + velocity) to avoid machine-perfect repetition; ghost-note support (low-velocity in-between hits)
Tempo-synced effects: tape/analog delay (1/4, dotted-1/8) and reverb sends
Tape/vinyl emulation: wow & flutter (slow pitch-modulation LFO, few cents) + continuous crackle/hiss layer that can run independent of the sequencer
Bitcrusher / sample-rate reducer (12-bit, variable downsample) for SP-1200/MPC crunch
Gentle/musical low-pass filter per track with envelope and slow open/sweep automation (for filter-up intros and filter-down breaks)
Sidechain compression keyed off the kick to duck chords/bass (with an exaggerated/'brutal' option that's stylistically acceptable)
Sample playback + chopping/repitch (for chord/melody/break samples), ideally with per-chop pitch and warp — much lo-fi is sample-based, not purely synth
Jazz-chord voicing engine: build maj7/min7/9/11/6-9/sus and altered-dominant voicings (with extensions and optional rootless/shell voicings) and 2-5-1 / I-vi-ii-V turnarounds from a key/mode
Slow harmonic-rhythm loop sequencer (1 chord per bar over 2-4-8 bar loops) with additive/subtractive arrangement automation (mute/unmute layers per phrase)
Mono bass handling with heavy low-pass and option for walking-bassline passing tones
Bus tape saturation/soft-clip and overall low-pass 'glue' on the master
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible to generate convincingly — arguably one of the easier electronic genres for an algorithm, because it's loop-based, minimal, low-tempo, and TOLERATES (even rewards) imperfection. The two genuinely hard parts are (1) the groove: a perfectly-quantized grid sounds sterile and instantly 'wrong', so the engine MUST do per-step micro-timing/humanization (snare early, kick/hat late, freehand drift) plus swing — get this and 80% of the authenticity follows; and (2) the 'dust' character: vinyl crackle, tape wow/flutter, gentle low-pass and bitcrush are easy DSP but must be layered tastefully or it sounds like a cheap filter, not a worn record. Jazz harmony is the third lift: maj7/min9/11 voicings and 2-5-1/I-vi-ii-V turnarounds need a real voicing engine, but a small curated chord-loop library covers most tracks. Sample-based authenticity (chopped breaks, sampled Rhodes/upright off old records) is the hardest to fully synthesize from scratch — a sample-playback path is the pragmatic shortcut. Sources are production-community consensus (Native Instruments, EDMProd, ModeAudio, LoFiWeekly) rather than a single canonical spec, so exact swing % and grid placement vary by ±a few percent / a step; I used the consensus ranges.

meter: 4/4 most common; also 3/4, 3/8, 6/8, 2/2 (cut time / alla breve), 9/8, 12/16. Bach gives essentially no tempo marks in the WTC (only one Andante/Largo in all 48), so tempo is implied by note-values and meter, not notated BPM.
feel: Straight, but with continuous-motion "spinning-out" (Fortspinnung): one steady pulse of running subdivisions (eighths or sixteenths) handed between voices so the texture never stops. NOT swung and NOT groove-quantized — micro-rubato and agogic stress, plus notes inegales optionally on stepwise eighth runs. There is no drum-driven feel; rhythmic interest comes from staggered voice entries and suspension/resolution.

scalesModes: Harmonic minor (raised leading tone for V and cadences)
Natural minor (Aeolian) fragments in descending lines
brief modal mixture / chromatic inflection in episodes
fully functional 24-key tonal system (the entire point of the Well-Tempered Clavier)
chordTypes: diminished 7th (vii°7) for intensification, esp. before final cadence
suspensions over triads producing 4-3, 7-6, 9-8, 2-3 dissonance-resolution figures
Neapolitan (bII6) and augmented-6th colorations near climaxes
typicalProgressions: Exposition tonic answer relation: i – (answer in) v, harmonized i – V; tonal answer bends scale-degree 5→5 back to 1→5 so the head stays in tonic before pivoting to dominant
Episode descending circle-of-fifths sequence: i – iv – VII – III – VI – ii° – V – i (each chord a falling 5th / rising 4th, 2-bar or 1-bar harmonic units), the workhorse modulating engine
Cadential drive: ii°6 – V7 – i (or IV – V7 – I in major); approach climactic cadences via vii°7/V
Middle-entry key plan (minor-key fugue): i → III (relative major, usual first middle entry) → v → iv → i; (major-key fugue): I → V → vi (relative minor) → IV → I
Final: tonic (or dominant) pedal point under stretto, then Picardy-third I in minor-key fugues
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Moderate-to-fast and continuous: typically one functional chord per beat or per half-bar in subject statements, accelerating to chord-per-beat in episodic sequences and the final cadence. Harmony is implied by the moving lines (linear counterpoint) rather than blocked, so it changes wherever the voices form a new vertical sonority — often on every strong eighth.

form: Through-composed, NOT loop-based. Three large zones: (1) EXPOSITION — each voice enters in turn with Subject (tonic) then Answer (dominant), alternating S-A; 2-voice = S,A; 3-voice = S,A,S (with a bridge/codetta to re-anchor tonic before the 3rd entry); 4-voice = S,A,S,A. A recurring Countersubject accompanies entries in invertible counterpoint. Optional counter-exposition (re-entries in reverse, inverted, or with subject+answer swapped). (2) DEVELOPMENT / MIDDLE ENTRIES — alternation of episodes (no full subject; sequential fragmentation of subject-head + countersubject, modulating, usually by descending circle-of-fifths) with middle entries of the subject in related keys (first middle entry typically relative major/minor, then subdominant/dominant). Devices: stretto (overlapping entries, answer before subject finishes), inversion, augmentation, diminution. (3) FINAL SECTION — return to tonic, often the tightest stretto and/or a tonic or dominant PEDAL POINT in the bass under busy upper voices, then a strong perfect-authentic cadence; minor fugues usually end on a Picardy-third major tonic. Typical length 20-60 bars (WTC fugues run ~27-115 bars).

bass.character: The bass is simply the lowest of the independent contrapuntal voices, not a separate riff layer. It is fully melodic: it states the Subject/Answer when its turn comes, carries the Countersubject elsewhere, and anchors functional harmony at cadences (roots of V-i). At the close it frequently sustains the tonic or dominant PEDAL POINT while upper voices spin counterpoint above. Range typically C2-C4 on harpsichord/organ.
bass.signatureTechnique: Walking quarter/eighth bass in episodes outlining the circle-of-fifths sequence; leaps of a descending 5th / ascending 4th between sequence roots; sustained pedal point under final stretto; provides the harmonic 'floor' that turns linear counterpoint into clear functional cadences.
bass.synthesis: Harpsichord (plucked, fast-decaying, bright, no dynamic accent), pipe organ (sustained, foot-pedal division), clavichord, or strings/bassoon in ensemble fugues. For a synth recipe: short-attack plucked tone — sawtooth or pulse through a static or slowly-closing lowpass, fast decay (~150-400 ms) with a thin sustain tail to emulate harpsichord; OR fully sustained additive/organ tone (stacked sine/square partials, no filter envelope) for organ fugues. NO sub-bass synth-bass character, NO sidechain, NO slide — it is a contrapuntal voice.

lead.character: There is no single 'lead' — the Subject is the protagonist and migrates through every voice, top to bottom, so leadership rotates. The Subject is a short, strongly-characterized melodic head (1-4 bars) with a memorable rhythmic-intervallic profile (e.g. a distinctive leap or a chromatic turn), followed by Fortspinnung running notes. All voices are timbrally identical (same instrument), so 'lead' = whichever voice currently carries the subject, made audible by register and rhythmic profile rather than by a different patch.
lead.synthesis: Same instrument/patch as every other voice (harpsichord or organ stop) — timbral uniformity is essential so the EAR tracks lines by pitch/contour, not by tone color. If using a synth: one fixed monophonic-capable patch instanced polyphonically across 2-4 voices (e.g. organ-like stacked square/sine, or plucked saw for harpsichord). Articulation: detache/legato per the line, light agogic emphasis on subject entries, no portamento.

pads.character: No pads in the EDM sense. The 'pad' equivalent is the sustained harmonic bed created by held notes and PEDAL POINTS — e.g. a long tonic or dominant note in the bass under the final stretto, or an organ's sustained inner voices. The harmony is a by-product of independent moving lines, not a chord-pad layer.
pads.synthesis: Pipe-organ sustain (additive stacked partials with stops/registration, slow chiff attack, gentle tremulant) gives the closest 'pad' surface; reverberant church/hall acoustic provides the wash. For a synth emulation: long sustained organ-like tone (sine + odd harmonics) for pedal points, generous hall reverb (2-4 s) for cathedral space. Avoid filter-sweep supersaw pads entirely — they break the period idiom.

drums.machine: NONE. The Baroque fugue is a purely melodic/contrapuntal genre with NO percussion or drum kit. 'Rhythm' is produced by the interlock of independent voices, staggered entries, suspensions, and continuous running subdivisions (Fortspinnung). Any drum machine (808/909/breakbeat) is stylistically wrong.
drums.kick: n/a — no kick. The functional analog of downbeat emphasis is the harmonic strong beat (chord change / cadence) and the metric accent implied by the meter.
drums.snare: n/a — no snare. Backbeat does not exist in this idiom.
drums.hats: n/a — no hats. The 'hi-hat' analog is the constant stream of running eighth/sixteenth notes passed between voices that keeps perpetual motion.
drums.canonicalPattern16: No percussion grid exists. If one must map the rhythmic FEEL to a 16-step bar (16 sixteenths, 4/4): continuous melodic motion fills the steps as a near-unbroken run of sixteenths (steps 1-16 all active in the moving voice), with the active step migrating between voices; metric/harmonic accents fall on steps 1 and 9 (beats 1 and 3) and secondarily 5 and 13 (beats 2 and 4); suspension dissonances are placed on strong steps (1,5,9,13) resolving down on the following weak step. There is NO kick/snare/hat layer to populate.
drums.other: Genuinely percussion-free. In rare orchestral/choral fugues a continuo or organ provides only harmonic/bass support, still no drums.

signatureSounds: Harpsichord (plucked, terraced dynamics, no expressive volume swell) — the WTC keyboard sound: pulse-wave/saw through a static bright lowpass, ~150-400 ms decay, no velocity-to-volume
Pipe organ with registration/stops — additive stacked partials (8'/4'/2' ranks = octave-stacked sines/squares), sustained, foot pedal-board for bass, optional tremulant, huge church reverb
Clavichord (intimate, faint Bebung vibrato) for smaller fugues
The Subject itself as a sonic signature — a 1-4 bar motif with a memorable head (often a leap of a 4th/5th or a chromatic descent) that the listener tracks through all voices
Suspension-and-resolution dissonance chains (4-3, 7-6, 9-8) as the characteristic harmonic 'tension' sound
Final tonic/dominant pedal point under a stretto pile-up = the climactic signature gesture
Picardy third — the unexpected major tonic ending a minor fugue
identityMustHaves: Imitative polyphony: a Subject stated alone, then imitated by 2-4 INDEPENDENT voices entering in turn (Subject in tonic, Answer in dominant)
Tonic–dominant subject/answer relationship with correct tonal-vs-real answer adjustment (bend scale-degree 5 when the subject opens on the dominant)
Episodes of sequential development (subject/countersubject fragments) that modulate by descending circle-of-fifths between middle entries in related keys
Strict functional-tonal voice-leading: no parallel 5ths/octaves, prepared-and-resolved suspensions, raised leading tone at cadences, contrary/oblique motion between voices
engineRequirements: TRUE POLYPHONIC MULTI-VOICE engine: 2-4 simultaneous independent melodic lines, each with its own note stream — NOT a single-scale monophonic step-sequencer per track

Subject/Answer transposition with tonal-answer logic: transpose subject by a 5th up / 4th down AND conditionally alter the head interval (scale-degree 5→1 mapping) when the subject begins on/emphasizes the dominant
Invertible counterpoint: ability to place the countersubject above OR below the subject (octave/double-octave inversion) and have it remain consonant

Functional-harmony voice-leading constraint solver: forbid parallel perfect 5ths/octaves, prefer contrary/oblique motion, prepare and resolve suspensions (dissonance on strong beat resolving down by step), raise the leading tone at cadences
Staggered/temporally-offset voice entries (each voice starts at a different bar) including STRETTO (overlapping entries at a controllable time offset before the prior entry finishes)
Motivic transformation operators: melodic inversion, augmentation (x2 durations), diminution (÷2 durations), fragmentation of the subject-head
Sequence/circle-of-fifths generator: take a motif and repeat it transposed down a 5th (or by a fixed scale interval) N times to drive modulation
Per-bar key/chord-context tracking so the same engine can render a voice correctly in tonic, relative major, subdominant and dominant key areas (multi-key, not single fixed scale)
Pedal-point support: one voice sustains/repeats a held tonic or dominant pitch while others continue
Through-composed arrangement (no loop): an exposition→development→final-section section planner, not bar-loop repetition
Cadence detection/placement to land perfect-authentic cadences and a Picardy third at the close
feasibilityNote: HARD — the most difficult genre to generate convincingly of any common style, and an extreme mismatch for a single-scale, monophonic, loop-based step-sequencer engine (as this project currently is per project_harmonic_architecture.md, where even chord/scale clashes between two independent tracks are an unsolved problem). A fugue requires (a) genuine multi-voice polyphony with independent simultaneous lines, (b) a real species-counterpoint/voice-leading constraint solver to avoid parallels and to prepare/resolve suspensions, (c) tonal-answer logic and motif transformation (inversion/augmentation/diminution/stretto), and (d) through-composed multi-key form rather than a looping single-scale grid. None of these are simple per-track parameter tweaks. A naive generator will produce parallel fifths, wrong answers, and aimless modulation that sounds 'wrong' to any listener familiar with the idiom. Realistic paths: hand-author a short fixed fugue as data (a curated preset, matching this project's 'fixed presets' strategy), OR template the structure (predefined subject + rule-based transposition for the exposition + canned circle-of-fifths episode + final pedal/stretto) and accept imperfect inner-voice counterpoint. Web sources were fully accessible; tempo, form, voice-entry order, answer logic, episode harmony and signature sounds are all directly grounded below.

genre: Synthwave / Outrun (a.k.a. Retrowave, Outrun, Futuresynth) — instrumental 1980s-nostalgia electronic music modelled on action/sci-fi/horror film soundtracks of the late '70s–'80s.
meter: 4/4 (straight, no compound meter; almost always 16-step-per-bar grid)
feel: Straight/quantized — robotic, near-zero velocity humanization. Slower "groovy" subset 80–118 BPM; driving "Outrun" subset 128–140 BPM with four-on-the-floor. Optional very light shuffle on hats only; the body is dead-straight machine timing.

scalesModes: Harmonic minor (for a darker, more dramatic raise-7 cadence)
Major / Ionian (the optimistic 'Retrowave' pop subset, e.g. A minor or B-flat major are the two most-cited home keys)
chordTypes: maj7 and min7 (the signature 'lush' extension — i-VI-VII often uses VImaj7)
add9 / sus2 / sus4 (drop or color the 3rd for that floaty 80s pad shimmer)
Occasional borrowed/chromatic chords: bII (Neapolitan), bVII, chromatic passing II
typicalProgressions: i - VI - III - VII (the quintessential minor-key synthwave loop, e.g. Am - F - C - G)
i - VI - VII (retro cascade, VI often maj7)
vi - IV - I - V and I - V - vi - IV (the pop/Retrowave major-key staples)
i - iv - VII - i (modal minor)
i - bVII - VI - bVII (descending darksynth)
i - bII - iv - V (noir / Neapolitan)
harmony.harmonicRhythm: Slow and hypnotic: most commonly 1 chord per bar (whole-note pads) across a repeating 4-bar loop, i.e. 4 chords per 4-bar phrase. Faster pop variants do 2 chords per bar. Pads hold sustained whole notes; harmony rarely changes mid-bar.

form: Loop-based, 4- and 8-bar building blocks. Canonical arrangement borrowed from 80s pop/film: 8-bar intro (often pad + arp only, or solo guitar), 16-bar verse, 8-bar pre-chorus/build, 16-bar chorus/'drop' (full kit + lead hook), 4-bar post-chorus, bridge/breakdown (drums drop out, pad swell + reverb wash), final chorus, outro fade. Key arranging device is SUBTRACTIVE: start sparse (kick+snare, no hats), add hi-hats, then arp, then lead as energy rises; strip back to pad-only for breakdowns. Drum builds use a snare/tom roll fill in the last bar of each 8-bar section to telegraph the section change.

bass.character: Plucky, chorused sawtooth (Korg Polysix / Juno / Jupiter-8 lineage) with a snappy filter-envelope. Two main modes: (1) driving root-note ostinato in straight 8ths or 16ths locked to the kick; (2) arpeggiated bass that walks chord tones with octave jumps (root - octave - fifth - octave) in 16ths. Resonant low-pass with a per-note filter-env 'pluck' is the defining timbre.
bass.signatureTechnique: 16th-note arpeggiated octave-jump bass with a fast filter-envelope pluck (decay ~0.2-0.3s, high resonance) and the bass sidechain-pumped to the kick. PWM and chorus add the 'bite'. Bass follows chord roots with minor passing-tone variation.
bass.synthesis: Saw (or square for punch) single/dual-osc monosynth; optional 2nd osc one octave down for weight; subtle PWM (square width LFO) for movement; 24dB low-pass filter with cutoff ~200-600Hz base + filter envelope adding ~3 octaves of sweep on each note (attack ~3ms, decay 0.25s, low sustain ~0.1); resonance cranked (Q ~8-12) for the resonant pluck; Juno-style chorus and light saturation. In the existing Tone.js engine this is a sawtooth MonoSynth, filterEnvelope baseFrequency 200 / octaves 3-4, fast decay, sidechained to kick.

lead.character: Bright, piercing, monophonic and 'whistleable' — a simple catchy hook of just a few notes, frequently with cheesy vibrato and portamento/glide between notes. Two flavours: a single fat detuned-saw lead, or a fast octave-jump arpeggio. Slap-back/eighth-note delay and big plate reverb place it in an 80s cinematic space.
lead.synthesis: Sawtooth (or square) — often two detuned saws (~10-20 cent spread, count 2-3) for thickness; alternatively the classic FM/DX7 bell or 'brass' tone. Low-pass filter mostly open with a quick filter-env. Add LFO vibrato (~5-6Hz, light depth) for the 'cheesy' wobble, portamento/glide ~50-100ms for slides, and tempo-synced slap-back delay (1/8 or 1/8-dotted) plus plate reverb. Double-track and hard-pan two takes (or two slightly detuned voices L/R) for width. Keep it monophonic and sparse — leads are mono note patterns, not chords.

pads.character: Thick, warm, lush analog polyphonic pad — the harmonic bed. Slow swelling attack, long release, washed in chorus and big reverb. The 'Juno-106 / Jupiter-8 / Prophet-5 strings-and-pad' sound. Holds sustained whole-note chords; common-tone voice-leading (keep shared notes between chords) for that floating, evolving wash.
pads.synthesis: Supersaw-style stack: 3-7 detuned sawtooth oscillators (count 3-7, ~12-30 cent / spread ~20-30 detune) per voice, polyphonic; low-pass filter with a SLOW filter-envelope opening (attack ~0.5-0.8s, release 1.5-2.4s, sustain high ~0.9, ~2.5 octaves of env sweep) so the pad blooms; heavy analog-style chorus (Juno/Jupiter ensemble — frequency ~0.6-1.2Hz, deep delay/depth) which is THE defining pad effect; large hall/plate reverb (size ~0.5-0.7, wet ~0.4); sidechain-pumped to the kick. Use sus2/add9 voicings to soften. In the existing engine: fatsawtooth count 3 spread 20-30, slow filterEnvelope, Tone.Chorus, reverb send, sidechain.

drums.machine: Classic 80s drum machines / samples: LinnDrum & Linn LM-1, Oberheim DMX, E-mu Drumulator, Simmons electronic toms, and Roland TR-808 (esp. its cowbell and toms) / TR-909. Sounds are sampled/electronic, not acoustic — punchy gated snare, big boomy toms, 808/909 kick.
drums.kick: Punchy electronic kick. In the driving Outrun variant: four-on-the-floor (every quarter). In the slower/groovy variant: kick on beats 1 and 3 (plus occasional 'and of 2' pickup). Tight, with short decay; sidechain source for the whole mix.
drums.snare: Big GATED-REVERB snare/clap on the backbeat — beats 2 and 4. This is the single most identifying drum element. Heavily compressed, often layered snare+clap, drenched in reverb whose tail is abruptly chopped by a noise gate (~hold 0.3-0.5s then hard release). Extra snare hits / rolls at bar-ends as fills.
drums.hats: Closed hi-hats fill the gaps between kick and snare in 8ths or 16ths (frequently on the offbeat 'ands'); open hi-hat accents on offbeats. Arrangement starts with NO hats (kick+snare only) and introduces hats as the track builds. Near-zero velocity variation — machine-flat.
drums.canonicalPattern16: 16 steps = one 4/4 bar (steps 1..16, beats land on 1,5,9,13). OUTRUN/driving variant: KICK on 1,5,9,13 (four-on-the-floor); SNARE+CLAP (gated reverb) on 5,13 (beats 2 & 4); CLOSED HAT on every offbeat 8th = steps 3,7,11,15 (or all 16ths 1-16 once built up); OPEN HAT on the 'and' offbeats 3,7,11,15 accenting; optional clap doubling on 5,13. SLOWER/groovy variant: KICK on 1,9 (beats 1 & 3) plus pickup on 8 ('and of 2'); SNARE on 5,13; HATS on 3,7,11,15. Last bar of each 8-bar section: snare/tom fill on 13,14,15,16 (16th roll) into the downbeat.
drums.other: Toms (big boomy Simmons/808 toms) for bar-end fills and tom rolls; TR-808 cowbell as a percussive ostinato hook; occasional crash on section downbeats. Whole kit typically routed to a gated-reverb send and bus compression/saturation.

signatureSounds: Gated-reverb snare on beats 2 & 4 — large reverb tail hard-chopped by a noise gate (the Padgham/Collins SSL technique, AMS RMX16 NonLin algorithm). THE defining sound.
Analog poly pad: detuned multi-saw stack through Juno/Jupiter-style chorus + big reverb, slow filter bloom (Juno-106 / Jupiter-8 / Prophet-5 lush pads).
Plucky chorused sawtooth bass — resonant filter-env pluck, often 16th-note arpeggiated with octave jumps (Korg Polysix / Juno lineage).
Bright monophonic saw or FM lead hook with cheesy vibrato, portamento glide, and slap-back delay (DX7 bells/brass or detuned saw).
16th/8th-note arpeggios (Jupiter-8 style arp) running under the lead — the hypnotic motor of the track.
TR-808 cowbell and big boomy electronic toms for fills.
Pumping sidechain compression — pads, bass and arps ducking to the kick for the 'breathing' 80s pump.
Sawtooth = bright leads, square = punchy bass, triangle/sine = mellow sub/pad — the textbook waveform assignment.
identityMustHaves: Gated-reverb backbeat snare/clap on 2 & 4 — without it, it doesn't read as synthwave.
Lush detuned analog-poly pad (chorused multi-saw stack) sustaining the chord bed.
Driving repetitive synth bass (root ostinato in 8ths/16ths or octave-jump arpeggio), locked to the kick.
Vintage 80s timbres throughout (Juno/Jupiter/Prophet/DX7 + LinnDrum/808) — sampled/analog character, dead-straight machine timing, minor-key (Aeolian) home with maj7/add9 color.
engineRequirements: Supersaw / unison detune stack (3-7 saws, per-voice cent spread ~12-30) for pads and leads
Per-note filter envelope with high resonance for the bass 'pluck' (fast attack/decay, multi-octave env amount)
Slow filter-envelope bloom on polyphonic pads (long attack/release, sustained whole-note voices)
Analog-style chorus/ensemble (Juno/Jupiter emulation) as a first-class effect on pads/bass/leads
Gated-reverb chain: reverb send -> noise gate keyed by the snare trigger, hard hold-then-release (~0.3-0.5s) — needs an envelope/gain node slammed open on each snare hit
Tempo-synced arpeggiator with octave-jump and up/down modes for bass and arp tracks (8th and 16th rates)
Sidechain compression / ducking of pads+bass+arp to the kick (LFO-style pump)
Portamento/glide + vibrato LFO on the mono lead for slides and cheesy wobble
Tempo-synced slap-back/eighth delay + large plate/hall reverb sends
Chord-aware voice-leading: snap melody/arp degrees to current chord tones, keep common tones between pad chords (sus2/add9 voicings)
16-step-per-bar sequencer with subtractive arrangement automation (mute/unmute tracks per section), near-zero velocity humanization, and bar-end fill/roll insertion
feasibilityNote: Highly feasible and largely already implemented in this repo's Tone.js engine (synthwave_surfer.html): it has fatsawtooth pad/lead/bass models with Juno-style chorus, a snare gated-reverb chain (Gain node slammed open per hit -> Reverb wet 1.0 -> hard close ~280ms), sidechain depth, filter envelopes, chord-aware degree snapping, and outrun/noir/dreamwave/carpenter generative profiles with i-VI-III-VII style padProgs. The genre's strength for generation is that it is loop-based, dead-straight, harmonically simple (1 chord/bar, 4-bar loops, minor + maj7), and timbre-driven — so a convincing result depends more on sound design (the gated snare, the chorused detuned pad, the resonant pluck bass) than on complex composition. The two hardest things to nail convincingly are (a) the gated-reverb snare transient/tail shape and (b) the lush analog chorus character; both are present here but benefit from tuning. Web access succeeded; specifics are grounded in the sources below, with production-community consensus favored where guides differed on exact BPM bands (80-118 groovy / 128-140 Outrun) and drum placement (kick 1&3 groovy vs four-on-floor Outrun).
